## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation for Freezing Time

**Background:** Proposed by **R. Plank (1913)** for calculating the time required to freeze a food material from an initial temperature to a final temperature, assuming an idealized geometry (infinite slab, infinite cylinder, or sphere).

**Assumptions (KTU-Favorite — Frequently asked):**
1. Uniform initial temperature throughout the food.
2. Constant surface temperature (or constant heat transfer coefficient).
3. Constant thermal conductivity ($k$) in the frozen zone.
4. Negligible sensible heat removal — only latent heat at the freezing interface is considered.
5. Thermal properties of the frozen layer are constant.
6. Slab geometry (or cylinder/sphere).

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Infinite Slab

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Consider a slab of thickness $2D$ (so half-thickness = $D$). The slab has:
- Initial temperature = $T_i$ (uniform).
- Freezing point = $T_f$ (assumed constant at the moving interface).
- Surface temperature = $T_s$ (or the medium is at $T_s$).

**Step 2 — Energy Balance:**
The rate of heat flow from the slab to the cold medium is the sum of:
- Surface heat transfer (convective resistance)
- Conduction through the frozen layer

$$q = \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}}$$

Where $x$ is the thickness of the frozen layer at any instant.

**Step 3 — Latent Heat Release at Interface:**
The rate of ice formation releases latent heat at the interface:

$$q = \rho \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$$

Where $\rho$ = density of food, $L$ = latent heat.

**Step 4 — Equate Both Expressions:**

$$\rho \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}}$$

**Step 5 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$\rho \cdot L \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}\right) dx = (T_f - T_s)\, dt$$

$$\int_0^D \rho L \left(\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}\right) dx = (T_f - T_s) \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Left Integral:**

$$\rho L \left[\dfrac{x}{h} + \dfrac{x^2}{2k}\right]_0^D = \rho L \left[\dfrac{D}{h} + \dfrac{D^2}{2k}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Equate to Right Side:**

$$\rho L \left[\dfrac{D}{h} + \dfrac{D^2}{2k}\right] = (T_f - T_s)\, t_f$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $t_f$:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{T_f - T_s} \left[\dfrac{D}{h} + \dfrac{D^2}{2k}\right]$$

**Step 9 — Introduce Plank's Geometric Parameters:**
Define:
$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{2}, \quad R = 1 \quad (\text{for infinite slab, simplest form})$$

Generalized form (Plank's Equation):

$$\boxed{\,t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{T_f - T_s}\left[\dfrac{P_a \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{2k}\right]\,}$$

For **infinite slab** (general form):
$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{2}, \quad R = 1$$

For **infinite cylinder**:
$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{4}, \quad R = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

For **sphere**:
$$P_a = \dfrac{1}{6}, \quad R = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biot Number (Bi) Correction for Slab** (for higher accuracy, $P_a$ depends on Bi):
> $$P_a = \dfrac{Bi}{2\,Bi + 1} \quad \text{where} \quad Bi = \dfrac{hD}{k}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

**Problem Statement:**
A slab of meat, **8 cm thick** (so $D = 4$ cm = 0.04 m), is to be frozen in an air blast freezer. Given:
- Density $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- Latent heat $L = 250$ kJ/kg = $250{,}000$ J/kg
- Initial temperature $T_i = 20$ °C
- Surface (freezer air) temperature $T_s = -30$ °C
- Freezing point $T_f = -2$ °C
- Surface heat transfer coefficient $h = 25$ W/m²·K
- Thermal conductivity $k = 1.5$ W/m·K

**Compute: Freezing time $t_f$ (in hours).**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Temperature Difference:**
$$\Delta T = T_f - T_s = -2 - (-30) = 28\text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Plank's Geometric Parameters for Infinite Slab:**
$$P_a = 0.5, \quad R = 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Plank's Equation:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P_a \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{2k}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**
$$\rho \cdot L = 1050 \times 250{,}000 = 2.625 \times 10^{8}\text{ J/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Bracket Term 1 (Surface Resistance):**

$$\dfrac{P_a \cdot D}{h} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.04}{25} = \dfrac{0.02}{25} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 6 — Bracket Term 2 (Conduction Resistance):**

$$\dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{2k} = \dfrac{1 \times (0.04)^2}{2 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{0.0016}{3.0} = 5.333 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 7 — Sum the Bracket:**

$$8.0 \times 10^{-4} + 5.333 \times 10^{-4} = 13.333 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 8 — Compute $t_f$:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{2.625 \times 10^{8}}{28} \times 13.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_f = 9.375 \times 10^{6} \times 13.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_f = 12{,}500\text{ seconds}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to Hours:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{12{,}500}{3600} \approx 3.47\text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** Notice the *surface resistance term* (0.0008) is comparable to the *conduction resistance term* (0.00053). This means **for thin slabs with low $h$**, improving the air velocity (raising $h$) reduces freezing time significantly. This is why **blast freezers** are preferred over static cold rooms.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the **freezing point** of a food material. Why does food freeze at a temperature *lower* than pure water?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The freezing point of a food material is the temperature at which the freezable water in the food begins to crystallize into ice, marking the onset of phase change.

Foods freeze at a temperature lower than pure water (0 °C) because they contain **dissolved solutes** such as salts, sugars, and organic acids. According to **Raoult's law**, the presence of solutes lowers the chemical potential of water, causing a **freezing point depression** quantified by:

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$

where $K_f = 1.86$ °C·kg/mol and $m$ is molality. Typical foods freeze between **−0.5 °C and −3.0 °C**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

List **three principles of food freezing** and explain why **quick freezing** is preferred over slow freezing.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Three principles of food freezing:
1. **Heat transfer driven process** — heat flows from food interior to cold medium, governed by $h$, $k$, and $\Delta T$.
2. **Phase change of water** — water (60–90% of food) crystallizes into ice, releasing latent heat of fusion (**334 kJ/kg**).
3. **Concentration of solutes in unfrozen phase** — as ice forms, the remaining liquid becomes concentrated, progressively lowering the freezing point.

Quick freezing is preferred because it forms **small intracellular ice crystals** that do not rupture cell walls. Slow freezing produces large extracellular crystals that damage cellular structure, leading to **high drip loss** (water and nutrients leak on thawing) and inferior texture. Quick freezing thereby preserves quality, color, and nutritional value.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **freezing point curve** of a food material and compare it with that of pure water. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A slab of fish, **10 cm thick**, is to be frozen in a blast freezer. Calculate the freezing time using **Plank's equation**, given:
- Density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³
- Latent heat $L = 235$ kJ/kg
- Initial temperature $T_i = 15$ °C
- Freezing point $T_f = -2$ °C
- Freezing medium temperature $T_s = -35$ °C
- $h = 30$ W/m²·K, $k = 1.6$ W/m·K
- $P_a = 0.5$, $R = 1$ (infinite slab)

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q A(a):**

The freezing point curve plots **temperature vs. time** for a food undergoing freezing. For pure water, the curve has a sharp **flat plateau at 0 °C**, since all water molecules freeze at a single temperature. Only two regions exist: sensible cooling and phase-change plateau.

For food materials, the curve has **three distinct regions**:

1. **Region A — Sensible Cooling:** From $T_i$ down to the initial freezing point $T_{ifp}$. Only sensible heat is removed: $Q_1 = m\,c_p\,(T_i - T_{ifp})$.
2. **Region B — Sloping Phase-Change Plateau:** Ice forms progressively, concentrating the remaining liquid. Temperature decreases slowly from $T_{ifp}$ to $T_e$ (eutectic / final freezing point). Latent heat dominates: $Q_2 = m\,L$.
3. **Region C — Sub-Cooling:** From $T_e$ down to the final freezer temperature $T_s$. $Q_3 = m\,c_{fs}\,(T_e - T_s)$.

| Comparison Aspect | Pure Water | Food Material |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Freezing Point | 0 °C (sharp) | -0.5 to -3 °C (sloped onset) |
| Plateau Shape | Flat horizontal | Sloped downward |
| Plateau End | All water frozen | Only free water frozen (~65–90%) |
| Number of Zones | 2 | 3 |
| Bound Water | None | Present, freezes below -20 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** The sloping plateau of foods is the most distinguishing feature compared to pure water — explicitly mention **solute concentration effect** for full marks.

---

**Model Solution for Q A(b):**

**Given:**
- $D = \dfrac{10}{2} = 0.05$ m (half-thickness)
- $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $L = 235{,}000$ J/kg
- $\Delta T = T_f - T_s = -2 - (-35) = 33$ °C
- $h = 30$ W/m²·K, $k = 1.6$ W/m·K
- $P_a = 0.5$, $R = 1$

**Step 1 — Plank's Equation:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P_a D}{h} + \dfrac{R D^2}{2k}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$\rho L = 1100 \times 235{,}000 = 2.585 \times 10^{8}\text{ J/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Surface Term:**

$$\dfrac{P_a D}{h} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.05}{30} = \dfrac{0.025}{30} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Conduction Term:**

$$\dfrac{R D^2}{2k} = \dfrac{1 \times (0.05)^2}{2 \times 1.6} = \dfrac{0.0025}{3.2} = 7.813 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — Bracket Sum:**

$$8.333 \times 10^{-4} + 7.813 \times 10^{-4} = 16.146 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $t_f$:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{2.585 \times 10^{8}}{33} \times 16.146 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_f = 7.833 \times 10^{6} \times 16.146 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$t_f = 12{,}648\text{ seconds} \approx 3.51\text{ hours}$$

**Answer:** Freezing time ≈ **3.51 hours**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **principles of food freezing** in detail with reference to heat transfer, phase change, and ice crystal formation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Plank's equation** for an infinite slab and discuss the geometric parameters $P_a$ and $R$ for slab, cylinder, and sphere geometries. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q B(a):**

Food freezing is governed by the following **five core principles**:

**1. Heat Transfer Driven Process:**
Freezing requires removal of thermal energy from food to a refrigerant medium. The rate of heat transfer is governed by **Fourier's law** (conduction inside food) and **Newton's law of cooling** (convection at surface). The overall rate-limiting factor is often the **surface heat transfer coefficient** ($h$), which depends on air velocity, contact, or immersion medium.

**2. Phase Change of Water:**
Foods contain 60–90% water. Freezing involves the conversion of this freezable water into ice crystals, releasing the **latent heat of fusion** ($L = 334$ kJ/kg for pure water; **~250 kJ/kg** for foods due to bound water effects). This phase change occurs at the freezing interface, which moves from the surface inward.

**3. Concentration of Solutes in the Unfrozen Phase:**
As water freezes, the dissolved solutes (salts, sugars) remain in the unfrozen liquid, making it **more concentrated**. According to Raoult's law, this progressively lowers the freezing point, producing the characteristic *sloping* plateau in the freezing curve of foods.

**4. Ice Crystal Size Depends on Freezing Rate:**
- **Slow freezing** → large extracellular crystals → cell rupture → drip loss.
- **Quick freezing** (blast, IQF, cryogenic) → small intracellular crystals → cell integrity preserved.

**5. Bound vs. Free Water:**
Not all water in food is freezable at standard storage temperatures. **Bound water** (held by proteins, starches) freezes only below **−20 °C**. Thus, even at **−18 °C**, ~10–35% of total water remains unfrozen.

**Engineering Implication:** Industrial freezers aim to maximize the freezing rate (high $h$) to ensure quick freezing — this is why **blast freezers, plate freezers, and cryogenic tunnels** are preferred over static cold rooms.

---

**Model Solution for Q B(b):**

**Derivation of Plank's Equation (Infinite Slab):**

Consider an infinite slab of half-thickness $D$, initially at $T_i$. The slab surface is exposed to a medium at $T_s$ (with $T_s < T_f$, the freezing point).

**Step 1:** Heat flows from the slab to the medium by surface convection and conduction through the frozen layer of instantaneous thickness $x$:

$$q = \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}}$$

**Step 2:** This heat is supplied by latent heat released at the ice front:

$$q = \rho L \dfrac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Equating:

$$\rho L \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{T_f - T_s}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}}$$

**Step 4:** Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x = D$ and $t = 0$ to $t = t_f$:

$$\rho L \int_0^D \left(\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{x}{k}\right) dx = (T_f - T_s) t_f$$

**Step 5:**

$$\rho L \left[\dfrac{D}{h} + \dfrac{D^2}{2k}\right] = (T_f - T_s)\, t_f$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $t_f$ and introducing generalized geometric constants $P_a$ and $R$:

$$\boxed{\,t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{T_f - T_s}\left[\dfrac{P_a D}{h} + \dfrac{R D^2}{2k}\right]\,}$$

**Geometric Parameters:**

| Geometry | $P_a$ | $R$ | Definition of $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infinite Slab | 1/2 | 1 | Half-thickness |
| Infinite Cylinder | 1/4 | 1/2 | Radius |
| Sphere | 1/6 | 1/3 | Radius |

**Discussion:**
- $P_a$ accounts for the *shape-dependent fraction* of heat flowing per unit surface area.
- $R$ accounts for the *additional thermal resistance* due to geometry.
- For a sphere, freezing is **fastest** (lowest $P_a$), and for a slab it is **slowest** per unit volume — but slabs are the simplest geometry for industrial plate freezers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong $D$ definition:** Students often use *full thickness* (10 cm) instead of *half-thickness* (5 cm) for slabs. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign errors in $\Delta T$:** Use $|T_f - T_s|$ or keep the larger minus smaller. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m, kJ and J. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting Biot correction:** For $Bi > 20$, $P_a \to 1/2$ for slab; for $Bi < 1$, $P_a \to Bi/2$. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert seconds to hours** when the question requests time in hours. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** of Plank's equation before deriving. Marks lost: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Freezing):**

- ☐ **Freezing** reduces food temperature *below* freezing point to form ice; **chilling** keeps it *above* freezing point.
- ☐ Standard frozen storage temperature: **−18 °C**.
- ☐ Freezing point of pure water = **0 °C**; most foods freeze between **−0.5 °C and −3 °C**.
- ☐ Freezing point depression: $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$, with $K_f = 1.86$ °C·kg/mol for water.
- ☐ The food freezing curve has **3 regions**: sensible cooling, sloping phase-change plateau, sub-cooling.
- ☐ **Slow freezing** → large extracellular ice crystals → cell damage → drip loss.
- ☐ **Quick freezing** → small intracellular ice crystals → better quality.
- ☐ **Bound water** (10–35%) never freezes at standard commercial freezer temperatures.
- ☐ **Plank's Equation (slab):** $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P_a D}{h} + \dfrac{R D^2}{2k}\right]$
- ☐ Geometric parameters: Slab ($P_a = 0.5$, $R = 1$); Cylinder ($P_a = 0.25$, $R = 0.5$); Sphere ($P_a = 1/6$, $R = 1/3$).
- ☐ For slab, $D$ = **half-thickness**; for cylinder/sphere, $D$ = **radius**.
- ☐ Latent heat of fusion of water = **334 kJ/kg**; effective latent heat of foods ≈ **200–260 kJ/kg**.
- ☐ Always use **SI units** consistently in Plank's equation.
- ☐ Increasing $h$ (blast freezing) reduces freezing time more effectively than lowering $T_s$ for thin products.
- ☐ Plank's equation assumes constant $k$, constant $L$, uniform $T_i$, and negligible sensible heat in the frozen zone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freezing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Freezing Systems # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Freezing** in food processing is defined as the unit operation in which the temperature of a food product is reduced below its **freezing point (T_f)** so that a significant fraction of the water present in the product is converted into ice crystals, with the simultaneous removal of the corresponding **latent heat of crystallization (~334 kJ/kg for pure water)**. The process is carried out under controlled thermal conditions to preserve nutritional, sensory, and microbiological quality of the food. A **Freezing System** refers to the engineered arrangement of **refrigeration plant, heat transfer surface, product handling mechanism, and control instrumentation** that delivers the required heat removal rate from the food to achieve a stable, deeply frozen (≤ −18 °C) end product. Key parameters defined by KTU standard metrics: - **Freezing Point of Food (T_f):** typically **−1 °C to −3 °C** (not 0 °C, due to solutes). - **Final Storage Temperature:** **−18 °C to −25 °C** (commercial cold chain). - **Latent Heat of Freezing (L):** ~**334 kJ/kg** of water. - **Specific Heat Above Freezing (C_pu):** ~**3.5 kJ/kg·K**. - **Specific Heat Below Freezing (C_pd):** ~**1.9 kJ/kg·K**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Slow-Down" Picture > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Food Freezing = Pressing the "Pause Button" on Life** Imagine a busy marketplace (this is your food — full of micro-organisms, enzymes, and chemical reactions all bustling around). When you **freeze** the marketplace, every shopper (molecule) is asked to stand still. The movement slows down so drastically that: - **Bacteria cannot reproduce** (their metabolism is essentially halted). - **Enzymes go into a dormant state** (chemical reactions slow by 10–100× for every 10 °C drop). - **Water turns into a solid lattice** — locked inside, unavailable for microbial growth. In simple words: **Freezing = removing heat until water becomes ice**, locking the food's freshness in time. The "trick" of an efficient freezing system is to do this **fast** (so ice crystals are small and don't rupture cells) and **uniformly** (so no part of the food is over- or under-frozen). --- ## 1.3 Why Food Freezing is Different from Water Freezing Pure water freezes sharply at **0 °C**. Food, however, contains salts, sugars, and proteins that **lower the freezing point** (a colligative property governed by Raoult's law). Therefore: > The freezing of food is a **progressive** process — water freezes gradually over a temperature range (typically **0 °C to −5 °C**), with about **80% of water converted to ice by −5 °C**. The un-frozen water remains in a **concentrated solution** with sugars and salts, and its freezing point is further depressed. This is the basis of the concept of **"bound water" and "freezable water"** in food systems. --- ## 1.4 Categorization of Freezing Systems (High-Yield Classification) | Category | Principle | Example | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical Refrigeration** | Vapor compression cycle | Air-blast, plate, spiral freezers | | **Cryogenic Freezing** | Direct contact with cryogen | LN₂ immersion, CO₂ tunnel | | **Indirect Contact** | Conduction through plates | Plate freezer, scraped surface | | **Direct Contact (Immersion)** | Liquid refrigerant bath | Brine immersion, glycol spray | | **Fluidized Bed** | Cold air suspends small particles | IQF (Individual Quick Freezing) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes **(i) Air-blast freezers, (ii) Plate freezers, (iii) Immersion freezers, and (iv) Cryogenic freezers**, along with **freezing time prediction using Plank's equation**. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Freezing — Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown The freezing of a food material proceeds through **three sequential regimes**: ### Stage A — Pre-cooling (Sensible Heat Removal Above T_f) The product is cooled from its initial temperature (e.g., 25 °C) down to the **initial freezing point (T_f)**. Heat removed here is **sensible heat**: $$Q_{pre} = m \cdot C_{pu} \cdot (T_i - T_f)$$ where $C_{pu}$ is the specific heat **above** the freezing point (~3.5 kJ/kg·K for most foods). ### Stage B — Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal at ~T_f) This is the **most energy-intensive step** (~60–70% of the total load). As water turns to ice, it releases **latent heat L ≈ 334 kJ/kg** of frozen water. Because the product's composition changes (ice fraction rises, solution concentrates), freezing actually occurs over a small temperature **band**, not a single point. $$Q_{latent} = m \cdot w \cdot L$$ where $w$ is the mass fraction of freezable water (typically 0.7–0.9 in meats and fish). ### Stage C — Sub-cooling (Sensible Heat Removal Below T_f) After most freezable water has crystallized, the remaining ice + concentrated solution is cooled to the **final storage temperature** (say −18 °C). Heat removed: $$Q_{sub} = m \cdot C_{pd} \cdot (T_f - T_{final})$$ where $C_{pd}$ is the specific heat **below** the freezing point (~1.9 kJ/kg·K). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Total Freezing Load** = Q_pre + Q_latent + Q_sub. The latent heat term **dominates** in most practical freezing calculations, which is why it is the focus of Plank's equation. --- ## 2.2 Heat Transfer Mechanisms Inside a Freezer Three modes operate **in series** (or in parallel) from the cold source to the product centre: 1. **Convection** — from cold air/refrigerant to the product surface. $q = h_i (T_s - T_\infty)$ 2. **Conduction** — through the product body (Fourier's law). $q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$ 3. **Phase change** — at the ice-front, requiring the supply/removal of latent heat. The **slowest step governs the rate** — and for thick products, **internal conduction** is almost always the rate-limiting step. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM-CRITICAL: Memorize this table. It contains every freezing equation a KTU examiner can ask.** | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | Validity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{pre} = m C_{pu} (T_i - T_f)$ | Sensible heat above freezing | kJ | Above $T_f$ | | 2 | $Q_{latent} = m w L$ | Latent heat of fusion | kJ | At $T_f$ band | | 3 | $Q_{sub} = m C_{pd} (T_f - T_s)$ | Sensible heat below freezing | kJ | Below $T_f$ | | 4 | $L_f = 334 + 2.05\, T_s$ | Latent heat of food ice (Heldman) | kJ/kg | $-40 \le T_s \le 0$ °C | | 5 | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\theta}\!\left[\dfrac{P D}{h_i} + \dfrac{R D^2}{k_f}\right]$ | **Plank's equation** | s | Slab, sphere, cylinder | | 6 | $P_{slab} = \tfrac{1}{2},\ R_{slab}=\tfrac{1}{8}$ | Plank's constants — slab | — | Infinite slab | | 7 | $P_{cyl} = \tfrac{1}{4},\ R_{cyl}=\tfrac{1}{16}$ | Plank's constants — cylinder | — | Infinite cylinder | | 8 | $P_{sph} = \tfrac{1}{6},\ R_{sph}=\tfrac{1}{24}$ | Plank's constants — sphere | — | Sphere | | 9 | $\theta = T_f - T_{air}$ | Temperature driving force | K or °C | Always positive | | 10 | $k_f \approx 1.25$ W/m·K | Thermal conductivity frozen food (typical) | W/m·K | Meat/fish | | 11 | $t_f = \dfrac{\Delta H_{10°C}}{ET_{max} \cdot E_{frozen}}$ | Pham's simplified method | s | Quick estimate | | 12 | $V_{refrig} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{h_{fg,ref} \cdot \eta}$ | Refrigeration plant capacity | kW | Plant design | > [!NOTE] > **Constants in Plank's equation:** > - $D$ = characteristic dimension (thickness for slab, diameter for cylinder/sphere). > - $h_i$ = **surface** heat transfer coefficient. > - $k_f$ = thermal conductivity of **frozen** layer. > - $\rho$ = density of food. > - $L$ = latent heat of the food. > - $\theta = T_f - T_{air}$ = temperature difference between initial freezing point and cooling medium. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cold chain logistics**: Freezing time dictates throughput, refrigerant cost, and product quality. - **IQF design**: Choosing the right air velocity (typically 2–6 m/s) is a balance between heat transfer and product dehydration. - **Plate freezer sizing**: Plate gap and hydraulic pressure govern capacity. - **Cryogenic tunnels**: Used for premium seafood and ready-meals where the freezing time is < 5 minutes. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Plank's Equation for Freezing Time of an Infinite Slab Plank's equation is the **cornerstone of KTU Module 3** and is mandatory for the 14-mark question. The derivation proceeds in **four clear stages**. ### Stage 1 — Define the Problem Geometry Consider an infinite slab of thickness $2D$ (i.e., thickness $D$ on each side from the centre plane). The slab is initially at its freezing point $T_f$. It is suddenly exposed to an air/cold medium at temperature $T_a$, where $T_a < T_f$. Heat is removed from **both faces** by convection with coefficient $h_i$, and conducted inward through the frozen layer to the receding **ice front** at $x = X(t)$. ### Stage 2 — Set Up the Energy Balance at the Ice Front The rate at which latent heat is liberated at the receding front must equal the rate at which heat is conducted **to the front** from the cold surface **plus** the rate at which the front is convectively cooled. Define $\theta = T_f - T_a$ (driving temperature difference). The two heat-flow terms are: **Conduction term** (Fourier's law through frozen layer of thickness $X$): $$q_{cond} = k_f \frac{\theta}{X}$$ **Convection term** (Newton's law at surface): $$q_{conv} = h_i \, \theta$$ ### Stage 3 — Combine into a Differential Equation for $X(t)$ The instantaneous rate of ice-front advance $\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ satisfies: $$\rho L \frac{dX}{dt} = k_f \frac{\theta}{X} - h_i \, \theta$$ Dividing both sides by $\theta$: $$\rho L \frac{dX}{dt} = \theta \left( \frac{k_f}{X} - h_i \right)$$ > Note: For freezing to proceed ($dX/dt > 0$), we must have $k_f / X > h_i$, i.e., the frozen layer is **still thin enough** that conduction can supply latent heat at the required rate. ### Stage 4 — Integrate from $X = 0$ to $X = D$ Separating variables: $$\int_0^D \frac{\rho L \, dX}{\theta \left( \dfrac{k_f}{X} - h_i \right)} = \int_0^{t_f} dt$$ Let $u = \dfrac{k_f}{X} - h_i$ → $du = -\dfrac{k_f}{X^2} dX$, so $dX = -\dfrac{k_f}{u^2} du$. After integration and simplification (substituting the integration limits and the geometric factors $P, R$), the result reduces to the celebrated **Plank's equation**: $$t_f = \frac{\rho L}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P D}{h_i} + \frac{R D^2}{k_f} \right)$$ with $P = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and $R = \tfrac{1}{8}$ for an infinite slab. **Same logic** (with different $P, R$) yields cylinder and sphere solutions. That is why **the constants P and R are the only things that change** between geometries — the form of the equation is universal. ### 3.1.1 Worked Numerical Example (Plank's Equation, Slab) > **Problem:** A 6 cm thick slab of fish (ρ = 1050 kg/m³, L = 235 kJ/kg, T_f = −2 °C) is placed in an air-blast freezer at T_a = −30 °C, with h_i = 25 W/m²·K and k_f = 1.25 W/m·K. Estimate the freezing time. **Step 1 — Convert all units to SI:** - $D = 0.06 / 2 = 0.03$ m (half-thickness) - $\theta = -2 - (-30) = 28$ K - $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³ - $L = 235{,}000$ J/kg - $h_i = 25$ W/m²·K - $k_f = 1.25$ W/m·K - $P = 0.5,\ R = 0.125$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Plank's equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_f &= \frac{(1050)(235{,}000)}{28} \left[ \frac{(0.5)(0.03)}{25} + \frac{(0.125)(0.03)^2}{1.25} \right] \\[4pt] &= (8{,}812{,}500) \left[ 6.0 \times 10^{-4} + 9.0 \times 10^{-5} \right] \\[4pt] &= (8{,}812{,}500) \left[ 6.9 \times 10^{-4} \right] \\[4pt] t_f &= 6080\ \text{seconds} \approx 1.69\ \text{hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The convection term (6.0 × 10⁻⁴) dominates over the conduction term (9.0 × 10⁻⁵) — meaning the **surface heat transfer** is the bottleneck, not internal conduction. Increasing $h_i$ (e.g., by raising air velocity) will reduce freezing time. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Freezing Point Depression (Why Foods Don't Freeze at 0 °C) For a dilute ideal solution, **Raoult's Law** gives the freezing-point depression: $$\Delta T_f = \frac{R \, T_f^{*2}}{\Delta H_{fus}} \cdot x_{solute}$$ For a multi-component food, an approximate engineering form is **Heldman's empirical correlation**: $$\Delta T_f \approx 1.86 \cdot m_{solute}$$ where $m_{solute}$ is the molality of dissolved solutes (mol/kg solvent). For typical meat (≈ 0.9% NaCl equivalent), this gives $\Delta T_f \approx 0.3$–$1.0$ °C, consistent with observed food freezing points of **−1 °C to −3 °C**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Latent Heat of Food Ice (Heldman / Singh) Because not all water freezes, the effective latent heat is **less than 334 kJ/kg**: $$L_f = w \cdot 334 \quad \text{(kJ/kg of food)}$$ A more precise temperature-dependent form, **Heldman-Denny**, uses: $$L_f = 334 + 2.05 \, T_s \quad \text{(kJ/kg ice)}$$ where $T_s$ is the storage temperature in °C. For $T_s = -18$ °C: $$L_f = 334 + 2.05(-18) = 334 - 36.9 = 297\ \text{kJ/kg ice}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Freezing Systems | Parameter | Air-blast | Plate | Immersion | Cryogenic (LN₂) | |---|---|---|---|---| | $h_i$ (W/m²·K) | 10–50 | 100–500 | 200–1000 | 500–2000 | | Freezing time (6 cm slab) | 1.5–3 h | 0.5–1.5 h | 10–30 min | 2–5 min | | Capital cost | Medium | High | Medium | High (consumable) | | Product shape | Any | Flat packages | Sealed packs | Small pieces | | Water-loss/dehydration | Yes (3–5%) | None | None | Negligible | | Energy efficiency | High (mech.) | Highest | High | **Lowest** (LN₂ costly) | | Typical KTU example | Frozen meat | Fish fillet blocks | Packed vegetables | IQF berries | --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Energy Demand of a Freezing Plant (Quick Sizing) Total plant capacity: $$V_{refrig} = \frac{Q_{total}}{h_{fg,ref} \cdot \eta_{cycle}}$$ If $Q_{total} = 200$ kW, refrigerant is **R-404A** with $h_{fg,ref} \approx 180$ kJ/kg, and $\eta = 0.45$ (typical COP), then: $$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{200}{180 \cdot 0.45} = 2.47\ \text{kg/s} \approx 8.9\ \text{tonnes/h}$$ --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Mechanical Freezing System (Vapor Compression Cycle) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional interaction** of a typical industrial food freezing system. The diagram is engineered to comply with the KTU 2024 assessment rubric of demonstrating system-level understanding. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
Vapor Compression] --> B[Condenser
Heat Rejection to Ambient] B --> C[Expansion Valve
Pressure Drop] C --> D[Evaporator Coil
Inside Freezer] D --> E[Cold Air Circulation
Fan and Ducts] E --> F[Food Product
Load on Trolleys or Belt] F --> G[Heat Transfer
Convection Plus Conduction] G --> D D --> A H[Control Panel
Temperature Sensors] -.->|Feedback| A H -.->|Feedback| E I[Defrost Cycle
Periodic Heating] -.-> D J[Product Out
Frozen at minus 18 degC] --> F ``` **How to read the diagram for the exam:** - The **compressor → condenser → expansion valve → evaporator** loop is the standard **vapor-compression refrigeration cycle**. - The **evaporator** is where the food actually loses heat. - The dashed lines represent **control signals** (sensors → PLC) and **auxiliary operations** (defrosting). --- ## 4.2 Classification Flow of Freezing Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Food Freezing Systems] --> M[Mechanical Refrigeration] S --> C[Cryogenic Freezing] M --> A1[Air Blast Freezer] M --> A2[Plate Contact Freezer] M --> A3[Fluidized Bed IQF] M --> A4[Spiral Freezer] C --> B1[Liquid Nitrogen LN2 Tunnel] C --> B2[Liquid CO2 Freezer] A1 --> P1[Batch Type] A1 --> P2[Continuous Tunnel] A3 --> Q1[Used for Peas Berries Diced Items] A4 --> Q2[Used for Pizzas Ready Meals] B1 --> R1[Premium Seafood and IQF Fruits] ``` **Key concept tested in KTU:** - **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing)** uses a **fluidized bed** where cold air suspends small product pieces — freezing in **5–15 minutes**, which is the fastest mechanical option. --- ## 4.3 Heat-Transfer Path Inside a Freezing Product (Ice-Front Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cold Air
T equals minus 30 degC] --> B[Convective Layer
h_i boundary] B --> C[Frozen Shell
Thickness grows with time] C --> D[Receding Ice Front
Releases latent heat] D --> E[Unfrozen Core
Concentrated solution] E --> F[Centre Plane
Last point to freeze] F -.->|Resistance controls rate| C ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall Note:** Many students forget that the **ice front moves inward** during freezing. The frozen shell acts as an **insulating barrier** — which is why freezing time scales with **D²** (not D). This is the physical reason for the $D^2$ term in Plank's equation. --- ## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture — Air-Blast vs. Plate vs. Cryogenic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AirBlast AB1[Air Fan] --> AB2[Refrigerated Coil] AB2 --> AB3[Cold Air Stream over Product] end subgraph PlateFrz PF1[Hydraulic Press] --> PF2[Refrigerated Hollow Plates] PF2 --> PF3[Direct Contact with Product] end subgraph CryoFrz CF1[LN2 Storage Tank] --> CF2[Insulated Tunnel] CF2 --> CF3[Spray or Dip of Product] end AirBlast --- PlateFrz --- CryoFrz ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Define freezing. How does freezing of food differ from the freezing of pure water?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Freezing** is a preservation technique in which the temperature of a food is reduced below its freezing point, converting most of its water content into ice and thereby suppressing microbial and enzymatic activity. > **Differences (2 marks):** > 1. **Freezing point:** Pure water freezes sharply at **0 °C**, whereas food freezes over a range of **−1 °C to −5 °C** because dissolved solutes (salts, sugars) **depress the freezing point** (a colligative effect). > 2. **Progressive phase change:** In water, freezing occurs at a single temperature. In food, ice forms **progressively** as temperature falls — about **80% of water** becomes ice by **−5 °C**, with the remainder held as bound water in a concentrated solution. > 3. **Quality impact:** Food freezing must control **ice-crystal size** (small crystals = less cell damage), which is not a concern for pure water. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]** **List any four commercial methods of food freezing. Mention one product for which each is most suitable.** **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form acceptable):** | # | Freezing Method | Suitable Product | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Air-blast freezer** | Meat carcasses, large fish | | 2 | **Plate contact freezer** | Fish fillets, packaged meat blocks | | 3 | **Immersion freezer** | Vacuum-packed chicken, fruits in syrup | | 4 | **Cryogenic (LN₂) freezer** | IQF berries, premium shrimp | | 5 | **Fluidized-bed (IQF) freezer** | Peas, diced vegetables, berries | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern:** Module 3 contains a **14-mark question with internal choice** between two questions A and B. You may attempt EITHER. Both are presented below for practice. --- ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted] (14 Marks)** #### (a) **Explain with a neat diagram the working of an air-blast freezer. What are its advantages and limitations? (7 marks)** #### (b) **A 5 cm thick slab of meat (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, L = 240 kJ/kg, k_f = 1.3 W/m·K, T_f = −2 °C) is frozen in an air-blast at T_a = −25 °C, with h_i = 30 W/m²·K. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation. Take P = ½, R = ⅛ for the slab. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a): 7 Marks** **Working of an Air-Blast Freezer (4 marks):** An air-blast freezer consists of a **tunnel or room** in which cold air (typically −25 °C to −40 °C) is circulated by **fans** over food placed on trolleys, racks, or a conveyor belt. The air is cooled by passing it over **refrigerated coils** containing a vaporizing refrigerant (e.g., R-404A, NH₃). Heat flows from the product to the air by forced convection, with surface heat transfer coefficient $h_i$ = 10 to 50 W/m²·K. **Key features to draw (or describe):** 1. Insulated enclosure. 2. Refrigerated evaporator coils. 3. Axial or centrifugal fans. 4. Product trolleys/conveyor. 5. Air ducts to direct flow over product. 6. Temperature sensors and control panel. 7. Optional defrost cycle. **Types of Air-Blast Freezers (1 mark):** - **Batch type** (stationary trolleys). - **Continuous tunnel** (product moves on a conveyor, counter-flow to cold air). - **Spiral** (uses a spiral conveyor to maximize product density per unit floor area). **Advantages (1 mark):** - Versatile — accepts products of any shape or size. - High throughput. - Lower capital cost than plate freezers. - Easy to integrate with IQF (fluidized bed). **Limitations (1 mark):** - **Surface dehydration** (3–5% weight loss) due to cold dry air. - **Non-uniform freezing** if airflow is poorly distributed. - **High energy consumption** (air is a poor heat-transfer medium). - Large floor-space footprint. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b): 7 Marks** **Given:** - Total thickness = 5 cm → **half-thickness D = 2.5 cm = 0.025 m**. - ρ = 1000 kg/m³, L = 240 kJ/kg = 240,000 J/kg. - k_f = 1.3 W/m·K, h_i = 30 W/m²·K. - T_f = −2 °C, T_a = −25 °C. - Plank constants: P = 0.5, R = 0.125. - θ = T_f − T_a = −2 − (−25) = **23 K**. **Step 1 — Write Plank's equation:** $$ t_f = \frac{\rho L}{\theta} \left[ \frac{P D}{h_i} + \frac{R D^2}{k_f} \right] $$ **Step 2 — Compute the bracket term (convection part):** $$ \frac{P D}{h_i} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.025}{30} = \frac{0.0125}{30} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the bracket term (conduction part):** $$ \frac{R D^2}{k_f} = \frac{0.125 \times (0.025)^2}{1.3} = \frac{0.125 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{1.3} = \frac{7.8125 \times 10^{-5}}{1.3} = 6.01 \times 10^{-5} $$ **Step 4 — Sum the bracket terms:** $$ \text{Bracket} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} + 6.01 \times 10^{-5} = 4.77 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the leading coefficient:** $$ \frac{\rho L}{\theta} = \frac{1000 \times 240{,}000}{23} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{8}}{23} = 1.043 \times 10^{7} $$ **Step 6 — Final multiplication:** $$ t_f = 1.043 \times 10^{7} \times 4.77 \times 10^{-4} = 4975\ \text{s} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{t_f \approx 4975\ \text{s} \approx 1.38\ \text{hours} \approx 83\ \text{minutes}} $$ **Comment (1 mark):** The convection term dominates (~87% of total time), indicating that **increasing air velocity** (raising $h_i$) is the most effective way to reduce freezing time for this product. --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted] (14 Marks)** #### (a) **Describe the working of a plate freezer and a cryogenic (LN₂) freezer. Compare their freezing times. (7 marks)** #### (b) **What is meant by 'Individual Quick Freezing' (IQF)? Explain the fluidized-bed IQF freezer with a diagram. State two products for which IQF is most suitable. (7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a): 7 Marks** **Plate Freezer (3 marks):** - A **plate freezer** consists of a stack of **hollow, refrigerated metal plates** arranged vertically or horizontally. - Packaged food (e.g., fish fillets, meat blocks) is placed between adjacent plates. - A **hydraulic ram** presses the plates together so that the food is in **direct contact** with the cold metal surface. - Refrigerant (NH₃ or R-404A) circulates inside the plates, cooling them to about −35 °C to −40 °C. - Heat is removed by **conduction** through the plate, giving a very high surface heat transfer coefficient ($h_i$ ≈ 100–500 W/m²·K). - **Freezing time:** 0.5 to 1.5 hours for a 6 cm slab (much faster than air-blast). **Cryogenic (LN₂) Freezer (3 marks):** - **Liquid nitrogen (LN₂)** boils at **−196 °C** at atmospheric pressure, providing an extremely cold heat sink. - Food is placed on a **conveyor** inside an **insulated tunnel**. - LN₂ is **sprayed** over the product, or the product passes through a **bath of LN₂**. - The product loses heat to the cryogen by **direct contact and condensation of nitrogen gas**. - Surface heat transfer coefficient: $h_i$ = 500 to 2000 W/m²·K (extremely high). - **Freezing time:** 2 to 5 minutes for small items (fastest method available). - Used for **premium seafood, IQF fruits, ready meals**. **Comparison (1 mark):** | Feature | Plate | Cryogenic | |---|---|---| | $h_i$ | 100–500 W/m²·K | 500–2000 W/m²·K | | Freezing time (6 cm) | 0.5–1.5 h | 2–5 min | | Capital cost | High | Medium (high running cost) | | Running cost | Low | **Very high** (LN₂ expensive) | | Suitability | Flat packs | Small premium pieces | --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b): 7 Marks** **Definition of IQF (2 marks):** > **Individual Quick Freezing (IQF)** is a freezing method in which **individual pieces** of a food product (e.g., peas, berries, shrimp) are frozen **separately and rapidly**, so that they do not clump together into a single frozen mass. This is achieved by passing the small pieces through a **fluidized bed of cold air**, which suspends each piece momentarily and freezes it within **5–15 minutes**. **Working of Fluidized-Bed IQF Freezer (4 marks):** 1. Small food pieces (typically 5–30 mm) are fed onto a **perforated mesh conveyor belt** at one end of an insulated tunnel. 2. **High-velocity cold air** (−30 °C to −40 °C, velocity 2–6 m/s) is blown **upward** through the perforated belt. 3. The upward air drag equals the weight of the particles → the bed becomes **fluidized**, with each particle individually suspended in the cold air stream. 4. Heat is removed rapidly by **forced convection** ($h_i$ = 200–500 W/m²·K), and each piece freezes individually in **5–15 minutes**. 5. The frozen product exits at the other end and is **packaged** without clumping. **Products for which IQF is most suitable (1 mark):** - **Green peas, sweet corn, diced carrots** (vegetables). - **Blueberries, strawberries, raspberries** (fruits). - **Shrimp, scallops** (seafood). - **Diced meat pieces** for ready meals. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to halve the thickness** — In Plank's equation, $D$ is the **half-thickness** for a slab, not the full thickness. Marks lost: 1–2. > 2. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing cm and m, or kJ and J. Always convert to SI **before** substituting. > 3. **Forgetting to specify Plank's constants P and R** — Examiners expect to see the values (½, ⅛ etc.) explicitly written. > 4. **Treating the problem as "pure water freezing"** — Food has a **depressed freezing point**; the initial temperature for sensible heat is $T_i$, not 0 °C. > 5. **Confusing $k_f$ (frozen) with $k_u$ (unfrozen)** — They differ by a factor of ~3. Always use the **frozen** value in Plank's equation because the ice front advances through the frozen layer. > 6. **Mislabelling the diagram** — In a Mermaid block, do not use reserved words like `end` or `subgraph` as standalone node IDs. Use `nodeE`, `subgA`, etc. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Pin this to your wall.** - [ ] **Freezing ≠ refrigeration.** Freezing converts water → ice; refrigeration may simply cool above 0 °C. - [ ] **Freezing point of food ≈ −1 °C to −3 °C** (not 0 °C). - [ ] **Three stages of freezing:** pre-cooling (sensible), phase change (latent, dominant), sub-cooling (sensible). - [ ] **Plank's equation** is the **#1 must-know equation**: $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\theta} \left[ \dfrac{P D}{h_i} + \dfrac{R D^2}{k_f} \right]$ - [ ] **Constants to memorize by heart:** - Slab: $P = ½,\ R = ⅛$ - Cylinder: $P = ¼,\ R = 1/16$ - Sphere: $P = ⅙,\ R = 1/24$ - [ ] **θ = T_f − T_air** (numerically the same in K or °C). - [ ] **Surface heat transfer coefficient order:** Air-blast (10–50) < Plate (100–500) < Immersion (200–1000) < Cryogenic (500–2000) W/m²·K. - [ ] **Freezing time scales with D²** — doubling thickness **quadruples** freezing time (because the frozen layer is the rate-limiting insulator). - [ ] **IQF = fluidized bed** — fast, no clumping, ideal for small pieces. - [ ] **Plate freezer** = highest efficiency for flat packages (no dehydration, fast heat transfer). - [ ] **Cryogenic freezing** = fastest method, but **expensive** due to LN₂ cost (~₹30–60 per litre). - [ ] **Quality rule:** Fast freezing → small ice crystals → less cell damage → better texture. Slow freezing → large crystals → drip loss on thawing. - [ ] **Heldman's latent heat** at −18 °C: $L_f \approx 297$ kJ/kg ice. - [ ] **Energy load order** for a freezing plant: $Q_{latent} > Q_{pre} > Q_{sub}$ for most foods. ---

topics.title: Direct Contact Systems, Air Blast Immersion
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Contact Systems, Air Blast Immersion

# Direct Contact Systems & Air Blast Immersion in Food Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Direct Contact Heat Transfer Systems** are thermal processing units in which the heat transfer medium (heating or cooling agent) is brought into **physical, intimate, and unmediated contact** with the food product, without any intervening solid wall, tube, or barrier. The heat exchange therefore occurs across a fluid–product interface governed entirely by convection (and sometimes mass transfer with simultaneous heat transfer).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, these systems are subdivided into:

1. **Air Blast Systems** – Where atmospheric or conditioned air is propelled at high velocity across the food surface using fans/blowers. Heat transfer occurs by forced convection, supplemented by evaporation when moisture is present.
2. **Immersion Systems** – Where the food product is physically submerged into a heated or cooled liquid medium (hot water, oil, brine, glycol, sugar solution, or liquefied cryogenic gas).
3. **Air Blast Immersion Systems** – A hybrid configuration in which the immersion bath is agitated by a continuous blast of air (or another gas) to enhance convective heat transfer coefficients, prevent localized hot/cold spots, and ensure product uniformity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Direct contact systems are a high-yield topic in Module 3 because they appear frequently as Part A (3-mark) definitional questions and as Part B (14-mark) descriptive + numerical problems involving the calculation of overall heat transfer coefficients and processing times.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are trying to cool down a hot bowl of soup quickly.

- **Indirect Contact (Heat Exchanger):** You pour the soup into a double-boiler pan. The heat must pass through the metal wall before reaching the cooling water. The metal wall acts as a "speed bump" slowing down the heat transfer.
- **Direct Contact (Air Blast):** You blow a desk fan directly at the soup surface. The moving cold air sweeps heat away rapidly by forced convection — there is no wall in the way.
- **Direct Contact (Immersion):** You drop a handful of ice cubes right into the soup. The ice touches the liquid directly and melts — an extreme form of direct contact.
- **Air Blast Immersion:** You drop the ice cubes AND stir the soup vigorously with a spoon (analogous to air blasting the bath). The combined action removes heat faster than any single method.

In all cases, **the absence of a separating wall means the overall heat transfer coefficient (U) collapses to the convective coefficient (h) on the product side**, dramatically increasing thermal efficiency.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Typical Air Heat Transfer Coefficient:** **h ≈ 10 – 100 W/m²·K** (blast air at 5–15 m/s)
- **Typical Water Immersion Coefficient:** **h ≈ 200 – 1000 W/m²·K**
- **Typical Hot Oil Immersion Coefficient:** **h ≈ 100 – 500 W/m²·K**
- **Specific Heat of Air (cp):** **1.005 kJ/kg·K** at 300 K
- **Specific Heat of Water (cp):** **4.186 kJ/kg·K** at 300 K
- **Thermal Conductivity of Air:** **0.026 W/m·K** at 300 K
- **Thermal Conductivity of Water:** **0.613 W/m·K** at 300 K
- **Forced Convection Air Velocity (Industrial):** **2 – 10 m/s** for cooling; up to **25 m/s** for blast freezing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The convective heat transfer coefficient **h** in direct contact systems is typically **5–50 times higher** than in indirect (jacketed) systems, which is why direct contact processing is preferred for time-sensitive food products such as blanching vegetables or blast freezing seafood.

### 1.4 Classification Diagram (Text-Based Overview)

Direct Contact Systems
├── Single-Phase Gaseous Media
│   ├── Air Blast Heating (e.g., drying ovens, roasting)
│   ├── Air Blast Cooling (e.g., chill rooms)
│   └── Air Blast Freezing (e.g., cryogenic & tunnel freezers)
│
├── Single-Phase Liquid Media
│   ├── Hot Water Immersion (e.g., blanching, pasteurization)
│   ├── Hot Oil Immersion (e.g., frying)
│   └── Cold Liquid Immersion (e.g., brine immersion chilling)
│
└── Hybrid: Air Blast Immersion
    ├── Air-agitated immersion blanchers
    └── Air-bubbled cryogenic immersion freezers

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Governing Principle

All direct contact heat transfer operations ultimately resolve to the **Newton's Law of Cooling/Heating**:

$$
Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lmtd}
$$

where:

- **Q** = rate of heat transfer (**W**)
- **h** = convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- **A** = effective heat transfer surface area (**m²**)
- **ΔT_lmtd** = log-mean temperature difference (**K**)

In direct contact systems, since there is no wall resistance, the overall heat transfer coefficient **U ≈ h**, where h is the convective coefficient on the product surface.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

#### Step A — Identify the Driving Force
- For **heating**: ΔT = T_medium − T_product
- For **cooling/freezing**: ΔT = T_product − T_medium
- The process is transient because the product temperature changes with time.

#### Step B — Select the Appropriate Correlation for h
Different geometries and flow regimes yield different Nusselt number (Nu) correlations. For KTU problems, the most commonly tested correlations are:

**1. Forced Convection over a Flat Plate (Air Blast on Conveyor Belts or Trays):**

$$
Nu_x = 0.664 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}
$$

(valid for laminar boundary layer; transition to turbulent at Re_x ≈ 5×10⁵)

**2. Forced Convection over a Sphere (Air Blast on Individual Food Pieces like Peas, Berries):**

$$
Nu = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}
$$

(Ranz-Marshall correlation — very high-yield for KTU)

**3. Forced Convection in Immersion (Sieder-Tate for liquids in tubes or around objects):**

$$
Nu = 0.027 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \left( \frac{\mu}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.14}
$$

#### Step C — Compute the Heat Transfer Coefficient
From the Nusselt number:

$$
h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D_c}
$$

where **k** = thermal conductivity of the medium and **D_c** = characteristic dimension (diameter for spheres, length for plates).

#### Step D — Apply the Transient Heat Transfer Equation
For an object with negligible internal resistance (lumped system analysis — Bi ≪ 0.1):

$$
\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\, t\right)
$$

where:

- **ρ** = density of food (**kg/m³**)
- **V** = volume of food (**m³**)
- **c_p** = specific heat of food (**J/kg·K**)
- **t** = time (**s**)

The dimensionless **Biot Number (Bi)** is the **single most important check** in any KTU numerical:

$$
Bi = \frac{h \cdot L_c}{k_f}
$$

where L_c = V/A (characteristic length) and k_f = thermal conductivity of the food. If **Bi < 0.1**, lumped analysis applies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application Domain | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = h A \Delta T_{lmtd}$ | All direct contact systems | W |
| Convective Coefficient from Nu | $h = Nu \cdot k / D_c$ | All forced convection cases | W/m²·K |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \rho v D_c / \mu$ | Determine flow regime | dimensionless |
| Prandtl Number | $Pr = c_p \mu / k$ | Fluid property grouping | dimensionless |
| Nusselt Number (Sphere) | $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ | Air blast on discrete particles | dimensionless |
| Nusselt Number (Plate) | $Nu_x = 0.664 Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ | Air blast on trays/films | dimensionless |
| Sieder-Tate (Immersion) | $Nu = 0.027 Re^{0.8} Pr^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Liquid immersion heating | dimensionless |
| Lumped System Equation | $\theta/\theta_i = \exp(-hAt/(\rho V c_p))$ | Bi $<$ 0.1 case | dimensionless ratio |
| Biot Number | $Bi = h L_c / k_f$ | Check lumped validity | dimensionless |
| Fourier Number | $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$ | Transient conduction depth | dimensionless |
| Process Time | $t = - \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A} \ln(\theta/\theta_i)$ | Heating/cooling time | s |
| Mass Transfer Analogy (Sherwood) | $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Sc^{1/3}$ | Drying with air blast | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Never confuse the thermal conductivity **k** of the food (used in Biot number) with the thermal conductivity of the heating medium (used in Nusselt number). They are different fluids and different contexts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System Type | Industrial Application | Why It Is Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Air Blast Freezing | Meat, poultry, seafood, ice cream hardening | Rapid surface heat removal; preserves cellular structure |
| Air Blast Cooling | Bakery products, post-cook chilling of fruits | Uniform, gentle convective cooling |
| Hot Water Immersion Blanching | Peas, carrots, potatoes | Inactivates enzymes; minimal nutrient loss |
| Hot Oil Immersion Frying | Potato chips, pakoras, samosas | Very high heat transfer for crisp texture |
| Cryogenic Immersion | IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) berries | Ultra-rapid freezing; small ice crystals |
| Air Blast Immersion | Continuous blanchers with air agitation | Combines immersion's high h with agitation uniformity |

### 2.5 Physical Mechanism in Air Blast Immersion

The hybrid **Air Blast Immersion** system works on three simultaneous mechanisms:

1. **Convective Heat Transfer** from the liquid medium to the food surface (h_liquid).
2. **Agitation Enhancement** by the rising air bubbles, which disrupt the thermal boundary layer around the food.
3. **Local Mixing** of the bulk liquid, preventing thermal stratification.

The effective coefficient becomes:

$$
h_{eff} = h_{liquid} \cdot (1 + f \cdot \epsilon)
$$

where **f** = an empirical agitation factor (typically 1.5 – 3.0) and **ε** = gas holdup (volume fraction of air in the liquid, dimensionless, typically 0.05 – 0.20).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lumped System Transient Equation

**Statement:** For a food particle with internal thermal resistance negligible compared to surface resistance (Bi < 0.1), derive the time required to change its temperature from T_i to a target T(t).

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Particle**

The rate of heat transfer INTO the particle equals the rate of enthalpy accumulation:

$$
Q = h A (T_\infty - T) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Separate Variables**

$$
\frac{dT}{T_\infty - T} = \frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Define Excess Temperature θ = T − T∞**

Note that dθ = dT since T∞ is constant. So the left side becomes:

$$
\frac{d\theta}{-\theta} = -\frac{d\theta}{\theta}
$$

The energy balance becomes:

$$
- \frac{d\theta}{\theta} = \frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from Initial State to Time t**

$$
\int_{\theta_i}^{\theta(t)} -\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = \frac{h A}{\rho V c_p} \int_0^t dt
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{\theta(t)}{\theta_i}\right) = -\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, t
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to Get the Working Form**

$$
\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\left( -\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, t \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve Explicitly for Time**

$$
t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}\, \ln\left( \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T(t) - T_\infty} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical "time to reach a target temperature" equation** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in Part B numerical problems.

### 3.2 Full Worked Numerical: Air Blast Cooling of a Meatball

**Problem Statement:** A spherical meatball of diameter **D = 0.04 m** is cooled in an air blast tunnel from an initial center temperature of **T_i = 80°C** to a target temperature of **T(t) = 15°C**. The air temperature is **T∞ = −10°C** and the air velocity is **v = 8 m/s**. Properties: k_air = 0.026 W/m·K, μ_air = 1.85×10⁻⁵ Pa·s, Pr_air = 0.71, ρ_air = 1.30 kg/m³, c_p,meat = 3500 J/kg·K, ρ_meat = 1050 kg/m³, k_meat = 0.50 W/m·K. Calculate the cooling time assuming lumped behavior is valid.

**Step 1 — Verify Lumped Assumption (Compute Biot Number)**

First compute h using the Ranz-Marshall correlation for a sphere:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.30 \times 8 \times 0.04}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 22{,}486
$$

$$
Pr = 0.71
$$

$$
Nu = 2 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Pr^{1/3}
$$

Compute Re^0.5:

$$
Re^{0.5} = \sqrt{22{,}486} \approx 149.95
$$

Compute Pr^(1/3):

$$
Pr^{1/3} = (0.71)^{1/3} \approx 0.892
$$

$$
Nu = 2 + 0.6 \times 149.95 \times 0.892 = 2 + 80.27 = 82.27
$$

$$
h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{D} = \frac{82.27 \times 0.026}{0.04} = 53.5\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
$$

For a sphere, characteristic length L_c = V/A = D/6:

$$
L_c = \frac{0.04}{6} = 0.00667\ \text{m}
$$

$$
Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k_{meat}} = \frac{53.5 \times 0.00667}{0.50} = 0.714
$$

Since Bi = 0.714 > 0.1, **lumped analysis is NOT strictly valid**. However, for an exam-level approximation (a common KTU-style simplification), we proceed with the lumped equation and acknowledge the error as an assumption.

**Step 2 — Compute the Geometric Parameters**

$$
V = \frac{\pi}{6} D^3 = \frac{\pi}{6} (0.04)^3 = 1.342 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = \pi D^2 = \pi (0.04)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Lumped Equation**

Temperature ratio:

$$
\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \frac{15 - (-10)}{80 - (-10)} = \frac{25}{90} = 0.2778
$$

$$
\ln(0.2778) = -1.2809
$$

Time coefficient:

$$
\frac{\rho V c_p}{h A} = \frac{1050 \times 1.342 \times 10^{-4} \times 3500}{53.5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{493.2}{0.2689} = 1834\ \text{s}
$$

Final cooling time:

$$
t = 1834 \times (-1) \times (-1.2809) = 2349\ \text{s} \approx 39.2\ \text{min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer for KTU board-style answer book:** t ≈ **2349 seconds (≈ 39.2 minutes)**.

### 3.3 Full Worked Numerical: Hot Water Immersion Blanching

**Problem Statement:** Carrot cubes of side **a = 0.02 m** are blanched in hot water at **T∞ = 95°C** from an initial temperature of **T_i = 20°C** to a target of **T = 75°C**. Water-side h = 800 W/m²·K. Properties of carrot: ρ = 1020 kg/m³, c_p = 3800 J/kg·K, k = 0.55 W/m·K. Verify lumped validity and compute blanching time.

**Step 1 — Compute Lumped Validity (Biot)**

For a cube, L_c = V/A = a/6 = 0.02/6 = 0.00333 m:

$$
Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{800 \times 0.00333}{0.55} = 4.85
$$

Bi = 4.85 >> 0.1. **Lumped analysis is invalid.** For KTU numerical credit, we either:

- **(Option A — Educational Simplification):** Apply lumped with a note saying "assuming lumped for approximation."
- **(Option B — Use Heisler Chart Approximation):** Look up Bi = 4.85 on a Heisler chart for a slab/sphere/cube.

For exam safety, the KTU board typically accepts **Option A** when explicitly stated.

**Step 2 — Lumped Time Calculation**

$$
V = a^3 = (0.02)^3 = 8 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = 6 a^2 = 6 \times (0.02)^2 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\frac{\rho V c_p}{h A} = \frac{1020 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 3800}{800 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{31.0}{1.92} = 16.15\ \text{s}
$$

Temperature ratio:

$$
\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \frac{75 - 95}{20 - 95} = \frac{-20}{-75} = 0.2667
$$

$$
\ln(0.2667) = -1.3218
$$

$$
t = 16.15 \times 1.3218 = 21.35\ \text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The dramatically shorter blanching time (≈ 21 s) compared to air blast cooling (≈ 39 min) **quantitatively demonstrates the superiority of immersion systems** — exactly the type of comparison KTU examiners love to ask in 14-mark Part B questions.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Air Blast Cooling | Hot Water Immersion | Air Blast Immersion (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Transfer Coefficient (h) | 30 – 100 W/m²·K | 500 – 1000 W/m²·K | 800 – 1500 W/m²·K |
| Process Time (typ.) | 20 – 60 min | 30 s – 5 min | 20 s – 3 min |
| Product Quality Impact | Minimal moisture loss | Possible leaching of solutes | Moderate |
| Energy Carrier Cost | Low (air is cheap) | Moderate (water heating) | High (both air + liquid) |
| Equipment Capital | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Best For | Delicate cooling | Rapid blanching | Uniform high-rate processing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Air Blast Cooling Tunnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fan Blower] --> A2[Heating or Cooling Coil]
    A2 --> A3[Duct Manifold]
    A3 --> A4[Air Blast Nozzles]
    A4 --> A5[Conveyor with Food Product]
    A5 --> A6[Recirculation Plenum]
    A6 --> A1
    A5 --> A7[Outlet to Packaging]
```
