## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define "Direct Contact Heat Transfer System" as applied to food processing. Give any two industrial examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A direct contact heat transfer system is one in which the heat transfer medium (heating or cooling fluid) is brought into **direct physical contact** with the food product, without any intervening solid wall or barrier. Heat exchange occurs across the fluid–product interface via convection (and sometimes simultaneous mass transfer).
**Industrial Examples:**
1. **Air blast freezer** used for rapid surface freezing of meat, fish, and poultry.
2. **Hot water immersion blancher** used for inactivation of enzymes in peas, carrots, and potatoes.
*(Any two valid examples accepted.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. State Newton's Law of Cooling and explain its significance in air blast cooling of foods.**

**Model Answer:**
Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of convective heat transfer between a fluid and a solid surface is directly proportional to the temperature difference and the heat transfer area:

$$
Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_\infty - T_s)
$$

where **h** is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), **A** is the surface area (m²), and **(T∞ − Ts)** is the temperature difference between the bulk fluid and the solid surface (K).
**Significance in Air Blast Cooling:** It enables direct estimation of cooling rate provided h is known (obtained from Nusselt correlations for forced convection). In air blast systems, h depends on air velocity, geometry of the food, and thermophysical properties of air.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the lumped system transient heat transfer equation for a food particle undergoing direct contact heating, starting from a basic energy balance. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical fruit of diameter 0.03 m is being cooled in an air blast tunnel. Initial temperature is 60°C, target temperature is 10°C, and the air temperature is −5°C. Air velocity = 6 m/s, k_air = 0.025 W/m·K, μ_air = 1.8×10⁻⁵ Pa·s, Pr = 0.71, ρ_air = 1.25 kg/m³. Fruit properties: ρ = 980 kg/m³, c_p = 3600 J/kg·K, k = 0.50 W/m·K. Compute (i) the heat transfer coefficient using Ranz-Marshall correlation, (ii) the Biot number, and (iii) the cooling time, assuming lumped behavior is valid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The derivation follows Section 3.1 of these notes, step by step:

1. **Assumption 1:** Bi < 0.1 (uniform temperature inside particle).
2. **Assumption 2:** Constant thermophysical properties (ρ, c_p, h).
3. **Assumption 3:** No internal heat generation.
4. **Energy balance:** Q into particle = Rate of enthalpy change.

$$
h A (T_\infty - T) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

5. Introduce excess temperature θ = T − T∞:

$$
-\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = \frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, dt
$$

6. Integrate from t = 0 to t:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{\theta(t)}{\theta_i}\right) = -\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, t
$$

7. Final form:

$$
\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, t\right)
$$

8. Solve for time:

$$
t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}\, \ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T(t) - T_\infty}\right)
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Compute h:**

$$
Re = \frac{1.25 \times 6 \times 0.03}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 12{,}500
$$

$$
Re^{0.5} = 111.80
$$

$$
Pr^{1/3} = 0.892
$$

$$
Nu = 2 + 0.6 \times 111.80 \times 0.892 = 61.84
$$

$$
h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D} = \frac{61.84 \times 0.025}{0.03} = 51.5\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
$$

**(ii) Compute Bi:**

$$
L_c = \frac{D}{6} = \frac{0.03}{6} = 0.005\ \text{m}
$$

$$
Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k_{fruit}} = \frac{51.5 \times 0.005}{0.50} = 0.515
$$

Since Bi > 0.1, lumped analysis is approximate.

**(iii) Cooling time:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi}{6} (0.03)^3 = 1.414 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
A = \pi (0.03)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \frac{10 - (-5)}{60 - (-5)} = \frac{15}{65} = 0.2308
$$

$$
\ln(0.2308) = -1.466
$$

$$
\frac{\rho V c_p}{h A} = \frac{980 \times 1.414 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600}{51.5 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{49.89}{0.1456} = 342.7\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t = 342.7 \times 1.466 = 502.4\ \text{s} \approx 8.37\ \text{min}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students mistakenly use the thermal conductivity of water instead of air in the Nusselt-to-h conversion, or forget to compute the Biot number check entirely. In the 14-mark problem, missing the Bi check loses **2 full marks**. Always show: (1) Compute Re, (2) Compute Pr, (3) Compute Nu, (4) Compute h, (5) Compute Bi, (6) Decide on lumped or distributed, (7) Apply the correct equation, (8) State the final answer with units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of an **air blast immersion blancher** used in the food industry. Discuss the role of air blasting in enhancing heat transfer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare air blast, hot water immersion, and hot oil immersion as direct contact heat transfer systems in food processing. Discuss at least four comparison parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Construction:**
An air blast immersion blancher consists of:
- A **rectangular stainless-steel tank** containing hot water maintained at 85–95°C by steam injection or electrical heaters.
- A **submerged perforated air manifold** running along the bottom of the tank, connected to a high-pressure blower that injects 5–25 m/s air jets into the water.
- An **inlet conveyor** at one end that submerges food pieces into the agitated bath.
- A **discharge conveyor** at the other end that lifts blanched product out for subsequent cooling.
- A **temperature control system** (PID) maintaining water temperature within ±1°C.
- A **water circulation pump** and filter for hygiene.

**Working:**
1. Food pieces (e.g., peas, diced carrots) are fed via the inlet conveyor.
2. They are submerged in the hot water bath.
3. The air manifold blasts bubbles upward, which:
   - Agitate the liquid and disrupt the thermal boundary layer around each particle.
   - Increase the convective heat transfer coefficient from ~600 W/m²·K (still water) to ~1200 W/m²·K.
   - Prevent thermal stratification and ensure uniform blanching.
4. The product is held in the bath for a calculated residence time (typically 1–5 minutes) to inactivate peroxidase and catalase enzymes.
5. The product exits through a dewatering conveyor and proceeds to a cooling section.

**Role of Air Blasting:**
- Enhances turbulence, raising h by a factor of 1.5 to 3.
- Prevents clumping of food pieces.
- Ensures uniform enzyme inactivation.
- Reduces overall processing time by 30–60%.

**Model Answer for (b):**

| Parameter | Air Blast | Hot Water Immersion | Hot Oil Immersion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Transfer Coefficient | 30 – 100 W/m²·K | 500 – 1000 W/m²·K | 100 – 500 W/m²·K |
| Process Time | Long (20 – 60 min) | Short (30 s – 5 min) | Very short (30 s – 3 min) |
| Product Effect | Gentle drying effect | Possible nutrient leaching | Adds fat content; crisp texture |
| Energy Efficiency | High (cheap medium) | Moderate | Low (oil heating costly) |
| Food Applications | Cooling, freezing, drying | Blanching, pasteurization | Frying |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In a "compare and contrast" question, students often list features without making **direct comparisons**. The KTU board examiner expects **at least four explicit comparison points**, each phrased as a sentence contrasting two systems. Bullet lists without comparative language lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Bullets for Last-Minute Reading:**

- **Direct Contact System** = Heat transfer medium touches food with NO intervening wall.
- **Air Blast** = Forced convection using high-velocity air; h ≈ 30–100 W/m²·K.
- **Immersion** = Food submerged in liquid; h ≈ 500–1000 W/m²·K.
- **Air Blast Immersion** = Hybrid system where air agitation enhances liquid immersion h to 800–1500 W/m²·K.
- **Governing Equation:** Q = h A ΔT (Newton's Law of Cooling).
- **Lumped System Validity Check:** Bi < 0.1.
- **Biot Number Formula:** $Bi = h L_c / k_{food}$ — **use food's k, NOT air's k**.
- **Characteristic Length for Sphere:** L_c = D/6.
- **Characteristic Length for Cube:** L_c = a/6.
- **Ranz-Marshall Correlation** (most frequently tested): $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ for spheres.
- **Lumped Time Equation:** $t = (\rho V c_p / hA) \ln[(T_i - T_\infty)/(T(t) - T_\infty)]$.
- **Always check units:** h in W/m²·K, A in m², ΔT in K, ρ in kg/m³, c_p in J/kg·K, t in seconds.
- **Industrial Applications to Memorize:**
  - Air Blast → Freezing, Cooling, Drying
  - Water Immersion → Blanching, Pasteurization
  - Oil Immersion → Frying
  - Air Blast Immersion → Continuous industrial blanching with agitation
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing thermal conductivity of food vs. medium in Biot and Nusselt respectively.
- **Why direct contact is preferred:** U ≈ h (no wall resistance), so processing is faster and energy-efficient.
- **Typical Air Properties to Memorize:** k_air ≈ 0.026 W/m·K, Pr_air ≈ 0.71, μ_air ≈ 1.85×10⁻⁵ Pa·s (at 300 K).
- **Typical Water Properties:** k_water ≈ 0.613 W/m·K, c_p = 4186 J/kg·K.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Changes in Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Changes in Foods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍎 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Changes in Foods* refer to the **physical, chemical, biochemical, and biological modifications** that occur in food components (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, vitamins, pigments, and water) during unit operations such as heating, cooling, drying, milling, mixing, and storage. These changes directly influence the **sensory quality, nutritional value, shelf-life, and consumer acceptability** of the finished food product.

## 🔍 The Four Pillars of Food Changes

| Change Type | Definition | Common Triggers |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Alterations in form, size, texture, or state without molecular transformation | Grinding, drying, freezing, mixing |
| **Chemical** | Reactions that modify the molecular structure of food constituents | Heat, light, oxygen, pH changes |
| **Biochemical** | Enzyme-mediated transformations within the food matrix | Cellular disruption, ripening, fermentation |
| **Biological** | Changes caused by living organisms, mainly microbes | Contamination, improper storage |

## 💡 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> **Think of food like a freshly cut apple slice left on the kitchen counter.**
> 
> 1. **Physical change**: The slice gradually **shrinks and shrivels** as water evaporates (moisture loss).
> 2. **Chemical change**: The slice turns **brown** due to oxidation of phenolic compounds reacting with oxygen.
> 3. **Biochemical change**: The cells still contain active **enzymes (polyphenol oxidase)** that catalyze the browning reaction even after cutting.
> 4. **Biological change**: Eventually, **bacteria and molds** colonize the slice, causing spoilage (fuzzy growth, off-odor).
> 
> In a food processing plant, we **control the timing, temperature, and environment** to either **encourage** desirable changes (baking bread, ripening cheese) or **inhibit** undesirable ones (preventing browning, stopping microbial growth).

## 🎯 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Water Activity (aw)** — dimensionless, ranges from **0.0 to 1.0**
  - Critical thresholds: **aw < 0.60** inhibits microbial growth
  - **aw > 0.85** supports most bacterial growth
- **Reaction Rate (k)** — governed by **Arrhenius equation**
- **pH values**: Most pathogenic bacteria thrive at **pH 4.5–7.0**
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** — measured in **°C or K**
- **Maillard Reaction** optimal at **aw ≈ 0.6–0.85**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:**
> The 2024 Scheme places heavy weight on understanding **WHY** these changes occur during unit operations — not just listing them. Always link the change back to the **specific processing step** (e.g., "during pasteurization, proteins denature because…").

## 🧬 Categories of Food Constituents Affected

1. **Carbohydrates** → Starch gelatinization, caramelization, hydrolysis
2. **Proteins** → Denaturation, coagulation, Maillard reaction with sugars
3. **Lipids** → Oxidation, hydrolysis, polymerization
4. **Vitamins** → Heat-labile destruction (especially Vitamin C, B-complex)
5. **Pigments** → Chlorophyll → pheophytin (green loss); Anthocyanin fading
6. **Water** → Evaporation, ice formation, bound vs. free water dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ A. Physical Changes in Foods

### 1. Change in **Texture & Rheology**

**Why it matters:** Texture determines mouthfeel and consumer preference.

- **Gelatinization** — Starch granules swell in water above **~60–70 °C**, losing crystalline order.
- **Retrogradation** — Re-association of amylose molecules upon cooling → bread staling.
- **Protein denaturation** — Unfolding of tertiary structure when heated above **~60–90 °C**.
- **Ice crystal formation** — Affects cell wall integrity during freezing.

$$ T_g \text{ (Glass Transition Temperature) — critical for dried/frozen food stability} $$

### 2. Change in **Color**

- **Browning Reactions:**
  - **Enzymatic browning** (cut apple, potato) — Catalyzed by **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)**, requires O₂.
  - **Non-enzymatic browning** — Two main types:
    - **Maillard Reaction** (amino acid + reducing sugar)
    - **Caramelization** (sugar alone, requires temperatures > **160 °C**)

### 3. Change in **Moisture Content / Water Activity**

The drying operation reduces moisture, moving the food toward a shelf-stable state.

### 4. Change in **Size & Shape (Shrinkage)**

During drying, food loses volume due to water removal → **shrinkage ratio** is a key quality index.

## ⚗️ B. Chemical Changes in Foods

### 1. **Maillard Reaction (The Star Topic of KTU)**

A multi-step reaction between **amino group (from proteins/amino acids)** and **carbonyl group (from reducing sugars)**.

**Three Stages:**
1. Initial stage → **Amadori compound** (no color yet)
2. Intermediate stage → **Colorless or yellow intermediates**
3. Final stage → **Melanoidins** (brown polymers, responsible for flavor/color of baked bread, roasted coffee)

**Factors Favoring Maillard Reaction:**
- pH **> 7** (alkaline)
- Temperature **> 50 °C**
- Water activity **0.6–0.85**
- Presence of **free amino groups + reducing sugars**

### 2. **Caramelization**

Requires **temperatures above ~160 °C** (sucrose: **160 °C**, fructose: **110 °C**).

**Three sequential stages:**
- Enolization
- Dehydration → forms diacetyl, furans
- Polymerization → brown pigments

### 3. **Lipid Oxidation (Rancidity)**

$$\text{Lipid (RH)} \xrightarrow{\text{O}_2, \text{heat, light}} \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Hydroperoxides (ROOH)} \rightarrow \text{Secondary products (aldehydes, ketones)}$$

**Types:**
- **Hydrolytic rancidity** → enzymatic breakdown by **lipases**
- **Oxidative rancidity** → produces off-flavors and toxic compounds

### 4. **Vitamin Degradation**

| Vitamin | Heat Sensitivity | Key Loss Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Vitamin C (Ascorbic acid) | **Very high** | Aerobic heating, light |
| Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) | High | Alkaline pH + heat |
| Vitamin A | Moderate | Oxidation, light |
| Vitamin D | Low | Relatively stable |

## 🦠 C. Biochemical Changes

Enzymes remain active in unblanched foods:

- **Pectic enzymes** → soften fruits (tomatoes, apples)
- **Proteases** → tenderize meat
- **Lipases** → hydrolyze fats in oilseeds
- **Polyphenol oxidase** → enzymatic browning

**Industrial Control:** **Blanching** (steam/water at 90–100 °C for 1–5 min) inactivates enzymes.

## 🧫 D. Biological Changes

| Agent | Effect |
|---|---|
| Bacteria | Spoilage, food poisoning (Salmonella, E. coli) |
| Yeasts | Fermentation (desirable in bread, wine) |
| Molds | Produce mycotoxins (e.g., **Aflatoxin** from *Aspergillus flavus*) |
| Insects | Mechanical damage, contamination |

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the consolidated high-yield formula table. All units and constants are KTU-exam-ready.

| # | Concept | Equation / Relation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Moisture Content (wet basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % | Drying calculations |
| 2 | **Moisture Content (dry basis)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$ | kg water/kg solid | Engineering design |
| 3 | **Water Activity Relation** | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH}{100}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Shelf-life prediction |
| 4 | **Arrhenius Equation** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | k in 1/s, 1/min | Rate of quality loss |
| 5 | **Quality Loss Kinetics** | $\ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right) = -kt$ | First-order | Vitamin retention |
| 6 | **D-value (decimal reduction time)** | $t = D \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | minutes | Sterilization |
| 7 | **Z-value (thermal death)** | $\log\left(\dfrac{t_2}{t_1}\right) = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{z}$ | °C | Microbial destruction |
| 8 | **F-value (sterilization value)** | $F = \int 10^{(T - T_{ref}) / z}\, dt$ | minutes | Canning operations |
| 9 | **Shrinkage Ratio** | $S_r = \dfrac{V_{dried}}{V_{fresh}}$ | Dimensionless | Drying quality |
| 10 | **Freezable Water** | $X_f = X_u - X_b$ | kg/kg dry | Bound vs. free water |
| 11 | **Half-life (1st order)** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Time units | Vitamin degradation |
| 12 | **Q10 (Temperature coefficient)** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$ | Dimensionless | Reaction rate vs. T |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The Q10 formula is **frequently tested**. Memorize that for many food reactions, **Q10 ≈ 2**, meaning a 10 °C temperature rise **doubles** the reaction rate.

---

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Change | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Bakery** | Maillard + Caramelization | Oven temperature profiling |
| **Dairy** | Protein denaturation, lactose browning | UHT vs. pasteurization |
| **Oil refining** | Lipid oxidation | Nitrogen blanketing, antioxidants |
| **Freeze-drying** | Glass transition, ice sublimation | Below Tg storage |
| **Canning** | Microbial inactivation | F-value calculations |
| **Blanching** | Enzyme inactivation | Time-temperature optimization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains complete, exam-grade derivations. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 1: Quality Loss Follows First-Order Kinetics

This derivation is **central to KTU problems** on vitamin retention, color degradation, and nutrient loss during processing.

### Step 1: Start with the rate equation

The rate of degradation of a quality attribute (e.g., Vitamin C concentration $C$) is proportional to its current concentration:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration at time $t$ (mg/100g)
- $k$ = reaction rate constant (1/min or 1/s)
- $t$ = processing time (min or s)

### Step 2: Separate variables

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt$$

### Step 3: Integrate both sides (from $C_0$ at $t=0$ to $C$ at $t=t$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} &= -k \int_{0}^{t} dt \\[4pt]
\left[ \ln C \right]_{C_0}^{C} &= -k \cdot t \\[4pt]
\ln(C) - \ln(C_0) &= -k \cdot t \\[4pt]
\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) &= -k \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Final Exponential Form

$$\boxed{\,C = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}\,}$$

or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\,\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k t\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation tells us that the **fraction retained** ($C/C_0$) decreases exponentially with time. A larger $k$ → faster degradation → shorter shelf-life.

---

## 🔢 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Q10 and Activation Energy

### Step 1: Apply Arrhenius at two temperatures

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)} \\
k_2 &= A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Take the ratio

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E_a/(R T_2)}}{e^{-E_a/(R T_1)}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

### Step 3: Simplify using common denominator

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}$$

### Step 4: Therefore

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right]$$

### Step 5: Take natural log

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a (T_2 - T_1)}{R \, T_1 T_2}$$

### Step 6: Solve for activation energy

$$\boxed{\,E_a = \frac{R \, T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This allows calculation of $E_a$ (in J/mol) from experimentally measured $k_1$ and $k_2$ at two known temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (in Kelvin).

---

## 🔢 Derivation 3: Moisture Content Conversion (Wet ↔ Dry Basis)

This is **frequently asked in KTU Part A** as a 3-mark question.

### Given:
- $MC_{wb}$ = moisture content on wet basis
- $MC_{db}$ = moisture content on dry basis
- $m_w$ = mass of water
- $m_d$ = mass of dry solid

### Step 1: Definitions

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$$

$$MC_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_d}$$

### Step 2: Express $m_w$ in terms of $m_d$ from dry-basis equation

$$m_w = m_d \cdot MC_{db}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into wet-basis formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC_{wb} &= \frac{m_d \cdot MC_{db}}{m_d \cdot MC_{db} + m_d} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{m_d \cdot MC_{db}}{m_d(1 + MC_{db})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Final Boxed Conversion

$$\boxed{\,MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}\,} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{\,MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** Food engineers prefer dry basis because it is invariant to sample size. Always convert and **state which basis** you are using.

---

## 🔢 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### **Problem:**
A fruit pulp loses Vitamin C during pasteurization at 85 °C. The rate constant at 85 °C is $k = 0.042 \, \text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate the **percentage of Vitamin C retained** after **20 minutes** of pasteurization.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the first-order degradation equation:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -(0.042)(20) = -0.84$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the retention ratio:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{C_0} &= e^{-0.84} \\[4pt]
&= 0.4317 \quad (\text{using } e^{-0.84} \approx 0.4317)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage:
$$\text{Retention} = 0.4317 \times 100 = \mathbf{43.17\%}$$

**Step 5:** Verification by direct exponential form:
$$C = C_0 \cdot e^{-0.84} = 0.4317 \, C_0 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Answer: 43.17 % of Vitamin C is retained.**

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix: When Each Change Matters

| Unit Operation | Dominant Change | Quality Concern | Control Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blanching** | Biochemical (enzyme inactivation) | Over-blanching → nutrient loss | Optimize time-temperature |
| **Pasteurization** | Chemical (vitamin loss, Maillard) | Off-flavor in milk | HTST vs. LTLT |
| **Sterilization** | Chemical + Microbial | Color, texture, over-cooked | F-value control |
| **Drying** | Physical (shrinkage), chemical (oxidation) | Case-hardening, browning | Controlled humidity |
| **Freezing** | Physical (ice crystals) | Cell rupture, drip loss | Quick freezing |
| **Extrusion** | Chemical (Maillard, starch dextrinization) | Color, flavor generation | High-temp short-time |
| **Fermentation** | Biological + Biochemical | pH drop, acid production | Inoculum control |
| **Storage** | All four types | Shelf-life reduction | aw, T, pH control |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between enzymatic and non-enzymatic browning in foods. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Enzymatic Browning | Non-Enzymatic Browning |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | Requires enzyme **Polyphenol Oxidase (PPO)** | No enzyme; chemical reaction |
| **Substrate** | Phenolic compounds + O₂ | Reducing sugars + amino acids (Maillard) OR sugars alone (Caramelization) |
| **Temperature range** | Room temp – 40 °C optimal; inactivated > 70 °C | Requires > 50 °C (Maillard), > 160 °C (Caramelization) |
| **Examples** | Cut apple, potato, banana turning brown | Baked bread crust, roasted coffee, caramelized sugar |
| **Control** | Blanching, lemon juice (lowers pH), vacuum packaging | Control aw, temperature, time |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
**Define Water Activity (aw). Mention its significance in food preservation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:**
Water activity is the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food ($p$) to the saturation vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature.

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$

where **ERH** = Equilibrium Relative Humidity (in %) of the air surrounding the food.

**Significance in Food Preservation:**
1. **Determines microbial growth:** Bacteria need **aw > 0.85**; most yeasts grow at **aw > 0.80**; halophilic bacteria down to **aw ≈ 0.75**; xerophilic molds down to **aw ≈ 0.65**.
2. **Governs chemical reactions:** Maillard reaction peaks at **aw ≈ 0.6–0.85**.
3. **Predicts shelf-life:** Lowering aw → longer stability (basis of drying, sugar/salt curing).
4. **Controls enzymatic activity:** Most enzymes require **aw > 0.6**.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme provides a choice. You must attempt either **Question A** or **Question B** fully. Each carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Maillard Reaction** in foods. Discuss the stages, influencing factors, and its importance in food processing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vegetable juice contains **25 mg/100 g of Vitamin C**. During pasteurization at **80 °C**, the rate constant is **k = 0.035 min⁻¹**. Calculate:
   1. The Vitamin C concentration after **30 minutes**.
   2. The percentage of Vitamin C retained.
   3. The **half-life** of Vitamin C under these conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

### Part (a) — Maillard Reaction Explained (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Maillard Reaction is a non-enzymatic browning reaction between **reducing sugars** (carbonyl group, –CHO) and **amino compounds** (–NH₂ from proteins, peptides, or free amino acids), producing characteristic brown pigments (melanoidins) and flavor compounds.

**Three Stages (3 marks):**

1. **Initial Stage (Carbonyl-Amine Condensation):**
   - Reducing sugar + amino acid → Schiff base → **Amadori rearrangement product** (1-amino-1-deoxy-2-ketose).
   - No color yet, but reaction is irreversible.

2. **Intermediate Stage:**
   - Dehydration and fragmentation of the Amadori compound.
   - Produces **colorless or yellow intermediates** — deoxyosones, furfurals, reductones.
   - Formation of **flavor volatiles** (pyrazines, thiophenes).

3. **Final Stage:**
   - Aldol condensation and polymerization.
   - Formation of **melanoidins** (brown, high-molecular-weight nitrogenous polymers).
   - Responsible for roasted, baked flavors and brown color.

**Influencing Factors (2 marks):**

| Factor | Optimal Condition for Reaction |
|---|---|
| Temperature | **> 50 °C**; doubles every 10 °C rise (Q10 ≈ 2–3) |
| Water activity | **0.6 – 0.85** (peaks here) |
| pH | **Alkaline (pH > 7)** favored; acidic conditions inhibit |
| Substrate type | Pentoses > hexoses; lysine-rich proteins most reactive |
| Time | Extended processing → more browning |

**Importance in Food Processing (1 mark):**
- **Desirable:** Bread crust, roasted coffee, seared meat, malted barley.
- **Undesirable:** Browning of milk powder during storage, off-flavor in sterilized milk, reduced nutritional quality (lysine destruction).
- **Control:** Reduce aw, lower temperature, shorten time, sulfite addition.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 25 \, \text{mg}/100\text{g}$
- $k = 0.035 \, \text{min}^{-1}$
- $t = 30 \, \text{min}$

**1. Concentration after 30 minutes (2 marks):**

Using first-order equation:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

Substituting:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{25}\right) = -(0.035)(30) = -1.05$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{25} &= e^{-1.05} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3499 \quad (\text{since } e^{-1.05} \approx 0.3499)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$C = 25 \times 0.3499 = \mathbf{8.75 \, mg/100g}$$

**2. Percentage retained (2 marks):**

$$\text{\% Retention} = \frac{C}{C_0} \times 100 = 0.3499 \times 100 = \mathbf{34.99\%}$$

**3. Half-life (3 marks):**

For first-order reactions:
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

Substituting:
$$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.035} = \mathbf{19.8 \, min}$$

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **physical and biochemical changes** that occur in foods during processing. Highlight the role of **blanching** in controlling enzymatic changes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **block diagram of changes in foods** during thermal processing and explain how **water activity** influences microbial and chemical stability. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

### Part (a) — Physical & Biochemical Changes + Blanching (7 Marks)

**Physical Changes (3 marks):**

1. **Change in texture:**
   - **Starch gelatinization:** Granules swell at 60–70 °C, water absorption increases, viscosity rises.
   - **Protein denaturation:** Unfolding of polypeptide chains; loss of solubility.
   - **Gel formation:** Pectin (in jam making), agar, gelatin.

2. **Change in color (physical aspect):**
   - **Caramelization** of sugars > 160 °C.
   - **Pigment degradation** — chlorophyll → pheophytin (green → olive brown); anthocyanins fade with heat.

3. **Change in moisture / volume:**
   - **Moisture loss** during drying.
   - **Shrinkage** — volume reduction in proportion to water removed.
   - **Freeze concentration / ice formation** in frozen foods.

4. **Change in size and shape:**
   - Mechanical operations like cutting, milling alter particle size and shape distribution.

**Biochemical Changes (2 marks):**

- **Enzymatic browning:** Catalyzed by PPO on phenolic substrates (e.g., apple, potato).
- **Starch breakdown:** By **amylases** → maltose, dextrins.
- **Lipid hydrolysis:** By **lipases** → free fatty acids → rancidity.
- **Proteolysis:** By **proteases** → peptides, amino acids.
- **Pectin breakdown:** By **pectin methylesterase (PME)** & **polygalacturonase (PG)** → softening of fruits.

**Role of Blanching (2 marks):**

**Definition:** Blanching is a short heat treatment (steam or hot water at 88–99 °C for 1–10 minutes) applied primarily to vegetables before further processing.

**Functions:**
1. **Inactivates enzymes** — destroys PPO, PME, catalase → prevents browning and texture loss.
2. **Removes air trapped in tissues** → improves vacuum packaging.
3. **Fixes color** in green vegetables by expelling intracellular gases.
4. **Reduces microbial load** (though not sterilization).
5. **Enhances drying rate** by softening tissues.

**Disadvantages of over-blanching:** Loss of heat-sensitive vitamins (C, B1) and texture softening.

---

### Part (b) — Block Diagram + Water Activity Role (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
┌─────────────────────┐
│  Raw Food Material  │
└──────────┬──────────┘
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Cleaning / Sorting  │  → Physical change (size reduction, removal of dirt)
└──────────┬──────────┘
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│     Blanching       │  → Biochemical change (enzyme inactivation)
└──────────┬──────────┘
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Thermal Processing  │  → Chemical change (Maillard, vitamin loss,
│ (Pasteurization /   │    protein denaturation)
│  Sterilization)     │
└──────────┬──────────┘
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Packaging & Storage │  → Biological control (microbial growth prevention
│                     │    via aw control)
└─────────────────────┘
```

**Water Activity — Role in Microbial & Chemical Stability (4 marks):**

**Definition:**
$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$

**Microbial Stability (2 marks):**

| aw Range | Microbial Growth |
|---|---|
| **1.00 – 0.95** | Pseudomonas, Salmonella (most Gram-negative bacteria) |
| **0.95 – 0.91** | Most spoilage bacteria |
| **0.91 – 0.87** | Most yeasts |
| **0.87 – 0.80** | Most molds, Staphylococcus aureus |
| **0.80 – 0.75** | Halophilic bacteria, xerophilic molds |
| **0.75 – 0.60** | Xerophilic yeasts |
| **< 0.60** | No microbial growth (sterile with respect to microbes) |

**Chemical Stability (2 marks):**
- **Maillard reaction** peaks at **aw 0.6–0.85** → highest browning risk.
- **Lipid oxidation** maximum at **aw ≈ 0.0–0.3** (dry foods) and decreases with increasing aw up to a minimum around aw ≈ 0.3–0.5.
- **Enzymatic reactions** require **aw > 0.6** (mostly).

**Conclusion:** Selecting an **intermediate aw** (e.g., 0.4–0.5 in dry foods) minimizes both microbial and chemical deterioration — a key engineering decision in product design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing moisture content on wet vs. dry basis** — always declare the basis. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting units** — write k in min⁻¹, t in minutes. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** — mention "first-order kinetics assumed", "isothermal conditions". (–0.5 to –1 mark)
> 4. **Missing the link between processing and change** — examiners expect you to say "during blanching, PPO is denatured, thus preventing enzymatic browning", not just list. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Mixing up Maillard with Caramelization** — Maillard needs AMINO acids; Caramelization needs only sugar + high heat.
> 6. **Calculating % retention as 100 - (% loss)** when formula already gives the ratio directly — double-counting error. (–1 mark)
> 7. **Ignoring significant figures** — match them to input data (e.g., 3 sig figs for 0.035 min⁻¹).

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Four categories of changes**: Physical, Chemical, Biochemical, Biological — know one example each.
- ⭐ **Water activity threshold**: aw < 0.60 = microbially stable; aw 0.6–0.85 = Maillard danger zone.
- ⭐ **Maillard Reaction** = Sugar + Amino acid → Melanoidins (brown); pH alkaline, aw 0.6–0.85.
- ⭐ **Caramelization** = Sugar alone, **> 160 °C**, no amino acid needed.
- ⭐ **Enzymatic Browning** = PPO enzyme + O₂ + phenolics → brown; controlled by blanching, lemon juice.
- ⭐ **First-order kinetics** for quality loss: $\ln(C/C_0) = -kt$.
- ⭐ **Half-life**: $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$.
- ⭐ **Arrhenius equation**: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$.
- ⭐ **Q10 ≈ 2** for most food reactions.
- ⭐ **Lipid oxidation** = Hydroperoxides → Aldehydes/Ketones (off-flavor).
- ⭐ **Vitamin C** is the most heat-labile vitamin — critical for pasteurization problems.
- ⭐ **D, z, F values** — essential for sterilization calculations in canning.
- ⭐ **Blanching** = 88–99 °C, 1–10 min, destroys enzymes (not microbes).
- ⭐ **Glass transition temperature (Tg)** controls freeze-dried food stability.
- ⭐ **Shrinkage** = direct result of moisture loss in drying.
- ⭐ **Aflatoxin** from *Aspergillus flavus* on improperly stored grains/nuts.
- ⭐ Always state assumptions in numerical problems.
- ⭐ Link every change to a **specific processing step** for full KTU marks.

---

**End of Module 3 — Changes in Foods | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Frozen Food Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Frozen Food Properties # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Frozen Food Properties** refer to the collective set of **thermophysical**, **rheological**, **structural**, and **physico-chemical attributes** that govern the behavior of food systems when their thermal energy is reduced to a level where a significant fraction of freezable water (typically > 75 %) transitions from the liquid phase into the solid (ice) phase. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OEFTT611), this topic is studied to predict, control, and optimize the design of freezing equipment, refrigerated storage, and cold chain logistics for foods such as meat, fish, fruits, vegetables, and dairy products. In food process engineering, the following property classes are considered: | Property Class | Key Parameters | |---|---| | Thermophysical | Specific heat, latent heat, enthalpy, thermal conductivity, density | | Phase-change | Freezing point, freezing point depression, glass transition (Tg) | | Mechanical/Rheological | Texture, hardness, shear strength, modulus of elasticity | | Structural | Cell-wall rupture, ice crystal size, porosity | | Chemical/Biochemical | Water activity (aw), pH shift, enzymatic activity, microbial inactivation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 outcome requires the student to *describe, calculate, and interpret* the physical and thermal properties of foods *before, during, and after* freezing. Numerical problems on **Plank's equation** and **enthalpy-based freezing time prediction** are *high-frequency* in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of freezing like pouring molten wax into a mould.** When hot liquid wax is poured into a cold metal mould, the wax does **not** instantly become solid everywhere. A solid "shell" forms first at the cold surface and progressively moves inward. The boundary between solid and liquid wax — called the **freezing front** — moves inward, and the rate of its movement depends on how fast heat is removed. Food freezing behaves **identically**: - The **freezing front** moves from the cold surface toward the thermal center of the food. - Heat is removed in **three stages**: 1. **Sensible cooling** (chilling the food from initial temperature to freezing point). 2. **Phase-change (latent heat removal)** — the longest and most critical stage; ~ **80 %** of the total refrigeration load occurs here. 3. **Sub-cooling** (cooling the ice + unfrozen water mixture to the final storage temperature). Just as the wax mould's properties (thermal conductivity, specific heat) determine the casting time, the **thermophysical properties of food determine the freezing time**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world analogy — Ice cubes vs. meat block:** A water ice cube freezes in ~2 hours at −18 °C. A beef steak of the same dimensions needs ~6–8 hours. **Why?** Because beef is ~ 70 % water, but the remaining 30 % (proteins, fats, salts) depresses the freezing point and changes the **apparent specific heat** and **thermal conductivity**. This is *why* food properties are central to freezing design. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and units are **universally used** in KTU 2024 scheme problems: - Latent heat of fusion of pure water: **L = 333.4 kJ/kg** (or **80 kcal/kg**) - Specific heat of water (above freezing): **cw ≈ 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** - Specific heat of ice: **cice ≈ 2.10 kJ/(kg·K)** - Density of water at 0 °C: **ρw ≈ 1000 kg/m³** - Density of ice at 0 °C: **pice ≈ 917 kg/m³** - Thermal conductivity of water: **kw ≈ 0.56 W/(m·K)** - Thermal conductivity of ice: **kice ≈ 2.24 W/(m·K)** - Cryoscopic constant for water: **Kf = 1.86 K·kg/mol** > [!NOTE] > These constants are **NOT** to be memorized by heart for KTU. The KTU question paper normally supplies the value in the data section of the problem. However, knowing the **order of magnitude** is essential for sanity-checking your numerical answer. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermophysical Properties — Step-by-Step Logic ### A. Specific Heat (cp) The specific heat of food is the heat required to raise 1 kg of food by 1 K. It is **highly temperature-dependent**, especially in the freezing zone. - **Above the initial freezing point (T > Tif):** Food behaves as a liquid solution. cp is calculated by mass-weighted summation of water, protein, fat, carbohydrate, ash, and salt. - **At the freezing zone (Tif > T > Tg):** cp becomes *apparent* because part of the heat removed is **sensible** (cooling ice/water) and part is **latent** (freezing more water). This is called the **apparent specific heat (capp)**: - The maximum apparent specific heat occurs near the freezing point. - **Below glass transition (T < Tg):** cp becomes nearly constant and equals cp of the solid matrix. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU interpretation:** When the problem states "calculate the refrigeration load" or "enthalpy change," use the apparent specific heat concept for the freezing zone and the actual specific heat above and below it. --- ### B. Latent Heat of Fusion (L) Latent heat is the energy released when 1 kg of water solidifies to ice (at 0 °C) — **333.4 kJ/kg**. In food, the **effective latent heat** is less than 333.4 kJ/kg because: - Not all water freezes (some remains as bound/unfreezable water at typical storage temperatures). - Solutes depress the freezing point. Effective latent heat: $$L_{eff} = L \cdot X_w \cdot (1 - X_b)$$ Where: - $X_w$ = mass fraction of water in food - $X_b$ = mass fraction of bound (unfreezable) water - $L$ = latent heat of pure water > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** $L_{eff}$ is the *true* heat load that must be removed to freeze the product. A food with high bound-water content (e.g., starch gels) has lower $L_{eff}$ and freezes faster. --- ### C. Enthalpy (h) Enthalpy is the cumulative heat content. In KTU problems, enthalpy is referenced to a datum (commonly −40 °C for frozen foods, or 0 °C). - **Enthalpy above freezing:** $h = c_{p,above} \cdot (T - T_{ref})$ - **Enthalpy below freezing:** $h = c_{p,above} \cdot (T_{if} - T_{ref}) + L_{eff} + c_{p,ice} \cdot (T - T_{if})$ - **Enthalpy at the freezing point:** $h_{if} = c_{p,above} \cdot (T_{if} - T_{ref})$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The total **heat to be removed (refrigeration load)** during freezing = $h_{initial} - h_{final}$ (both referenced to the same datum). --- ### D. Thermal Conductivity (k) Thermal conductivity determines how fast the freezing front moves inward. - **Above freezing:** k ≈ 0.4–0.6 W/(m·K) (similar to water) - **Below freezing:** k ≈ 1.0–2.5 W/(m·K) (similar to ice) - **At the freezing zone:** k is intermediate and modeled as a function of the ice fraction. Siebel's model (commonly tested in KTU): $$k = k_{liq} \cdot (1 - x_{ice}) + k_{ice} \cdot x_{ice}$$ Where $x_{ice}$ is the mass fraction of ice. --- ### E. Density (ρ) - Water expands ~ **9 %** upon freezing (ρice < ρwater). - In food, the overall density also depends on the porosity and air content. - During freezing, the **apparent specific volume increases** (food may "swell") — critical for packaging design. --- ### F. Freezing Point & Freezing Point Depression Pure water freezes at 0 °C. Foods freeze at a *lower* temperature due to dissolved solutes. **Cryoscopic (Freezing Point Depression) Equation:** $$\Delta T_f = \frac{R \cdot T_f^2 \cdot x_2}{\Delta H_{fus} \cdot 1000}$$ Or in the simplified form: $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$ Where: - $K_f$ = 1.86 K·kg/mol for water - $m$ = molality of solutes (mol/kg solvent) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility:** The lower the initial freezing point (Tif), the longer the phase-change zone, the slower the freezing, and the larger the energy required. --- ### G. Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) The temperature below which the amorphous (unfrozen) matrix of food becomes a **rigid glass**. Tg depends on moisture content, composition, and molecular weight. - For low-moisture foods (cookies, dried fruits): Tg may be above 25 °C. - For high-moisture foods (meat, fish): Tg is around −10 °C to −30 °C. - Below Tg, mobility of molecules drops sharply → reactions, crystallization, and microbial growth are minimized. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability rule:** A food is **stable** when stored at $T < Tg$. Storage above Tg leads to caking, stickiness, recrystallization, and loss of texture. --- ### H. Water Activity (aw) and Ice Fraction The fraction of water frozen at any sub-zero temperature T can be estimated from the **water activity** (using the Clausius–Clapeyron relation for ice): $$x_{ice} = \frac{a_w - 1}{a_w \cdot \ln a_w} \quad \text{(approximate form)}$$ Or more simply, from the phase diagram of ice: $$a_w = \frac{p_{ice}(T)}{p_{water}(T_f)}$$ > [!NOTE] > As temperature falls, aw drops. At **−18 °C**, $a_w$ of frozen food is ~ 0.82; at **−30 °C**, $a_w$ ~ 0.75. This is the **fundamental reason** frozen storage preserves food — microbial growth is inhibited at $a_w < 0.86$. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Freezing point depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | $K_f = 1.86$ K·kg/mol, $m$ = molality | Dilute solutions only | | 2 | Effective latent heat | $L_{eff} = L \cdot X_w \cdot (1 - X_b)$ | $L = 333.4$ kJ/kg, $X_w, X_b$ mass fractions | $0 \le X_b < X_w$ | | 3 | Total refrigeration load | $Q = h_{initial} - h_{final}$ | $h$ in kJ/kg, Q in kJ/kg | Steady process | | 4 | Siebel's thermal conductivity | $k = k_{liq} (1 - x_{ice}) + k_{ice} \cdot x_{ice}$ | $k$ in W/(m·K), $x_{ice}$ mass fraction | Freezing zone | | 5 | Apparent specific heat (max) | $c_{app,max} = c_{water} + \frac{L}{T_{if} - T_{storage}}$ | $c$ in kJ/(kg·K) | Approximate max value | | 6 | Ice density vs. water density | $\rho_{ice} = 0.917 \cdot \rho_{water}$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³ | At 0 °C | | 7 | Plank's equation (infinite slab) | $t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{T_f - T_\infty} \left( \frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} \right)$ | $t$ in s, $a$ half-thickness in m | Constant $T_\infty$ | | 8 | Plank's equation (infinite cylinder) | $t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{T_f - T_\infty} \left( \frac{P \cdot r}{h} + \frac{R \cdot r^2}{k} \right)$ | $r$ = radius | | | 9 | Plank's equation (sphere) | $t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{T_f - T_\infty} \left( \frac{P \cdot D}{6h} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{24k} \right)$ | $D$ = diameter | | | 10 | Plank's shape factors | Slab: $P = 1/2, R = 1/8$; Cyl: $P = 1/4, R = 1/16$; Sph: $P = 1/6, R = 1/24$ | Dimensionless | | | 11 | Glass transition (Gordon–Taylor) | $T_g = \frac{w_1 T_{g1} + k \cdot w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k \cdot w_2}$ | $w$ = mass fractions, $k$ ~ 0.5–0.8 | Two-component systems | | 12 | Clausius–Clapeyron (sublimation) | $\ln \frac{p_2}{p_1} = -\frac{L}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Freeze-drying context | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU examiner tip:** When asked to calculate freezing time, **always state the shape factor** (P, R) explicitly *before* substituting. Marks are lost when students write the formula but forget to specify slab/cylinder/sphere. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility The properties discussed above are not abstract — they are the **backbone of cold-chain engineering**: - **Refrigeration load calculation** for cold storage warehouses (uses L_eff, cp, enthalpy). - **Freezer tunnel / blast freezer design** (uses Plank's equation). - **Packaging design** (uses density change, ice volume expansion). - **Shelf-life prediction** (uses water activity, glass transition, recrystallization kinetics). - **Texture & quality prediction** (uses ice crystal size, Tg, and structural collapse temperature). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Plank's Equation for an Infinite Slab **Assumption (as given in KTU data):** - Slab thickness = 2a (so a = half-thickness). - Both faces are cooled by a medium at constant temperature $T_\infty$. - Surface heat transfer coefficient = h (W/m²K). - Initial food temperature = $T_i$. - Initial freezing point = $T_f$. - Final center temperature = $T_c$ (or center reaches $T_f$). - Latent heat of freezing = L (J/kg). - Density = ρ (kg/m³). - Thermal conductivity of frozen layer = k (W/mK). - Constant properties assumed. **Step 1: Energy balance at the freezing front** The freezing front moves inward. The heat that must be removed in time *dt* is the latent heat released by the newly frozen layer of thickness *dx*: $$dQ_{latent} = \rho \cdot L \cdot A \cdot dx$$ **Step 2: Heat conducted through the frozen layer** By Fourier's law, the heat flow through the frozen layer of instantaneous thickness *x* is: $$Q_{cond} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_f - T_\infty)}{x}$$ **Step 3: Equate latent heat release to conduction** $$\rho \cdot L \cdot A \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_f - T_\infty)}{x}$$ **Step 4: Separate variables and integrate** $$\rho \cdot L \cdot x \cdot dx = k \cdot (T_f - T_\infty) \cdot dt$$ Integrating from x = 0 to x = a, and t = 0 to t = t_f (the freezing time): $$\int_0^a \rho \cdot L \cdot x \, dx = \int_0^{t_f} k \cdot (T_f - T_\infty) \, dt$$ $$\rho \cdot L \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = k \cdot (T_f - T_\infty) \cdot t_f$$ **Step 5: Solve for $t_f$ — pure conduction case** $$t_{cond} = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot a^2}{2 \cdot k \cdot (T_f - T_\infty)}$$ **Step 6: Add surface resistance term** The surface heat transfer resistance adds another term. The full form is: $$t_{f} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{(T_f - T_\infty)} \left[ \frac{a}{h} + \frac{a^2}{2k} \right]$$ Written in **Plank's generalized form** (with P and R shape factors): $$\boxed{\,t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{(T_f - T_\infty)} \left[ \frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} \right]\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > For an **infinite slab**: $P = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $R = \tfrac{1}{8}$ (using half-thickness *a*). For an **infinite cylinder**: $P = \tfrac{1}{4}$, $R = \tfrac{1}{16}$ (using radius *r*). For a **sphere**: $P = \tfrac{1}{6}$, $R = \tfrac{1}{24}$ (using diameter *D*). --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]:** A meat product (ρ = 1050 kg/m³, L = 235 kJ/kg, c = 3.6 kJ/(kg·K), k = 1.4 W/(m·K)) is frozen in a slab of half-thickness a = 0.04 m. Initial freezing point T_f = −2 °C, ambient T_∞ = −25 °C, and surface heat transfer coefficient h = 20 W/(m²K). Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation. **Solution:** Step 1: Identify the shape — infinite slab → P = 1/2, R = 1/8. Step 2: Substitute values into Plank's equation. $$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{(T_f - T_\infty)} \left[ \frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} \right]$$ Step 3: Unit-convert L: 235 kJ/kg = 235,000 J/kg. Step 4: Substitute numerical values: $$t = \frac{1050 \cdot 235000}{(-2 - (-25))} \left[ \frac{0.5 \cdot 0.04}{20} + \frac{0.125 \cdot (0.04)^2}{1.4} \right]$$ Step 5: Evaluate the terms. Numerator of the prefactor: $1050 \cdot 235000 = 2.4675 \times 10^8$ J/m³. Denominator of the prefactor: $(-2) - (-25) = 23$ K. Prefactor: $\dfrac{2.4675 \times 10^8}{23} = 1.0728 \times 10^7$ J/(m³·K). Step 6: Bracket term — surface resistance: $$\frac{0.5 \cdot 0.04}{20} = \frac{0.02}{20} = 0.001 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ Step 7: Bracket term — conduction resistance: $$\frac{0.125 \cdot 0.0016}{1.4} = \frac{0.0002}{1.4} = 1.4286 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ Step 8: Sum: $$0.001 + 1.4286 \times 10^{-4} = 0.00114286 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ Step 9: Final multiplication: $$t = 1.0728 \times 10^7 \cdot 0.00114286 = 12{,}260 \text{ s}$$ Converting to hours: $$t = \frac{12{,}260}{3600} \approx 3.41 \text{ hours}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** The surface term (0.001) is **larger** than the conduction term (1.43×10⁻⁴). This is typical for **air-blast freezing** where h is low (10–25 W/m²K). If we had **immersion freezing** with h = 500 W/m²K, the conduction term would dominate — and freezing would be limited by the *food's* conductivity, not the air-side resistance. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Refrigeration Load **Problem:** Calculate the total refrigeration load to freeze 1000 kg of fish from 20 °C to −20 °C. Data: - Water content = 0.75 - Initial freezing point = −1.5 °C - Bound water = 5 % of total water - $c_{above} = 3.6$ kJ/(kg·K) - $c_{ice} = 2.1$ kJ/(kg·K) - $L = 333.4$ kJ/kg **Solution:** Step 1: Effective latent heat: $$L_{eff} = 333.4 \cdot 0.75 \cdot (1 - 0.05) = 333.4 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.95 = 237.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 2: Sensible heat above freezing (20 °C → −1.5 °C): $$Q_1 = c_{above} \cdot (T_i - T_f) = 3.6 \cdot (20 - (-1.5)) = 3.6 \cdot 21.5 = 77.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 3: Latent heat removed at freezing zone: $$Q_2 = L_{eff} = 237.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 4: Sensible heat below freezing (−1.5 °C → −20 °C): $$Q_3 = c_{ice} \cdot (T_f - T_{final}) = 2.1 \cdot (-1.5 - (-20)) = 2.1 \cdot 18.5 = 38.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 5: Total heat per kg: $$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 77.4 + 237.55 + 38.85 = 353.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 6: For 1000 kg of fish: $$Q_{batch} = 353.8 \cdot 1000 = 353{,}800 \text{ kJ} = 353.8 \text{ MJ}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** The latent heat (237.55 kJ/kg) is **~67 %** of the total refrigeration load. This confirms the earlier statement that ~2/3 of the energy is removed in the freezing zone. Reducing freezing time → smaller ice crystals → better quality. But this is *thermodynamically* the slowest stage. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property → Engineering Decision | Property | Key Engineering Question Answered | Design Impact | |---|---|---| | Specific heat (cp) | How much energy to cool 1 kg by 1 K? | Determines refrigerant flow rate | | Latent heat (L) | How much energy to freeze the product? | Determines compressor sizing | | Thermal conductivity (k) | How fast does freezing front advance? | Determines freezing time | | Density (ρ) | How much volume does frozen product occupy? | Packaging & storage volume | | Freezing point (Tf) | At what temperature does phase change begin? | Determines refrigerant set point | | Glass transition (Tg) | At what temperature is the product shelf-stable? | Determines optimal storage T | | Water activity (aw) | At what T does microbial growth stop? | Determines freezer temperature | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Three Stages of Freezing ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Initial Food State
T = Ti above Freezing] --> B[Stage 1 - Sensible Cooling
Remove sensible heat cp dT] B --> C{Temperature = Tif
Freezing Point} C -->|Yes| D[Stage 2 - Latent Heat Removal
Water converts to Ice
L = 333.4 kJ per kg] D --> E[Stage 3 - Sub-cooling
Cool ice matrix to Tstorage] E --> F[Frozen Food at Storage
T less than Tg and Tstorage] B --> G[Heat Output to Refrigerant

Q3 = cice dT] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The branch labels (G, H, I) show *where* the heat goes. Note that branch **H (latent heat)** is the largest contribution to the refrigeration load — typically 60–80 % of the total. --- ## 4.2 Plank's Equation — Architecture of Energy Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Inputs direction TB A1[Food Geometry - slab, cylinder, sphere] A2[Thermophysical - rho, L, k, cp, h] A3[Process - Tf, Tinf, Ti] end subgraph Calculation direction TB B1[Step 1: Compute shape factors P and R] B2[Step 2: Compute delta T = Tf minus Tinf] B3[Step 3: Apply Plank equation] end subgraph Output direction TB C1[Freezing Time t in seconds] C2[Convert to hours for plant design] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B2 --> B3 B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Property Changes Through Freezing (Conceptual Map) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Before Freezing
Liquid Water dominates] --> P2[Freezing Front moves inward
Ice forms at cold surface] P2 --> P3[During Freezing
Ice + Unfrozen Solution coexist] P3 --> P4[Below Tg
Glassy amorphous matrix] P1 -. cp high .-> P1 P2 -. cp apparent maximum .-> P2 P3 -. Latent heat dominant .-> P3 P4 -. cp low constant .-> P4 P1 -. aw high near 1 .-> P1 P2 -. aw decreasing .-> P2 P3 -. aw 0.82 at minus 18 C .-> P3 P4 -. aw 0.70 to 0.75 .-> P4 P1 -. Density 1000 kg per m3 .-> P1 P2 -. Volume expansion 9 percent .-> P2 P3 -. Density 917 kg per m3 for pure ice .-> P3 P4 -. Density of frozen matrix .-> P4 ``` --- ## 4.4 Comparison of Freezing Methods (Effect on Properties) | Method | Typical h (W/m²K) | Dominant Resistance | Ice Crystal Size | Quality | |---|---|---|---|---| | Still air (−18 °C) | 5–10 | Surface | Large | Moderate | | Forced air (blast) | 15–25 | Surface | Medium | Good | | Plate contact | 50–100 | Conduction | Small | Very good | | Immersion (brine) | 200–500 | Conduction | Very small | Excellent | | Cryogenic (LN₂) | 500–1500 | Conduction inside | Tiny | Best (rapid) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering takeaway:** Higher *h* shifts the dominant resistance from the air-side to the food's internal conduction. Once h is large enough, **freezing time becomes limited by the food's thermal conductivity, not by the freezer's air supply**. This is the conceptual basis for *individually quick-frozen* (IQF) products. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define glass transition temperature (Tg). Why is it important in frozen food storage?** **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):** The **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature below which the amorphous (non-crystalline) components of food — primarily sugars, starches, and proteins — transition from a rubbery, flexible state to a rigid, glassy state. Below Tg, molecular mobility is severely restricted, which suppresses diffusion-limited reactions such as **recrystallization, enzymatic browning, and caking**. **Importance in frozen food storage (2 marks):** 1. Foods stored at $T < Tg$ exhibit **maximum shelf-life** because chemical, physical, and microbial deteriorative reactions are minimized. 2. If stored at $T > Tg$, the matrix remains rubbery → ice crystals grow (recrystallization) → texture degrades and water may migrate (freezer burn). > [!NOTE] > **Valuation tip:** The examiner will check whether the student wrote a *definition* (1 mark) AND *consequence* of exceeding Tg (2 marks). A vague "Tg is the temperature at which food becomes glass" without explaining *what* changes (molecular mobility) will lose at least 1 mark. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **List any four thermophysical properties of food that change significantly during freezing.** **Model Answer (4 items × 0.75 mark each = 3 marks):** 1. **Specific heat (cp):** increases sharply to an apparent maximum in the freezing zone, then drops to a low value for fully frozen food. 2. **Thermal conductivity (k):** increases from ~0.5 W/(m·K) above freezing to ~1.5–2.5 W/(m·K) below freezing as water converts to ice. 3. **Density (ρ):** decreases because ice (ρ ≈ 917 kg/m³) is less dense than water (ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³), leading to volume expansion of ~9 %. 4. **Latent heat of fusion (L):** apparent latent heat is released only in the freezing zone; the effective L is less than 333.4 kJ/kg due to bound/unfreezable water. 5. *(Bonus — water activity)*: aw drops from ~1.0 (above freezing) to ~0.82 at −18 °C and ~0.75 at −30 °C. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model - Part B]** **(a)** Define the **initial freezing point** of food. Explain the concept of **freezing point depression** in food systems with the help of the cryoscopic equation. (7 marks) **(b)** Derive **Plank's equation** for the freezing time of an infinite slab, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Definition (2 marks):** The **initial freezing point (Tif)** of food is the temperature at which the first ice crystals begin to form in the food matrix. Unlike pure water, food is a multi-component solution containing salts, sugars, acids, and proteins; therefore Tif is always **lower** than 0 °C. **Freezing Point Depression (5 marks):** In a solution, the chemical potential of water is reduced by dissolved solutes. To restore equilibrium with the solid (ice) phase, the system must be cooled to a lower temperature. This is called **freezing point depression**, denoted $\Delta T_f$. The **cryoscopic equation** is: $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$ Where: - $K_f = 1.86$ K·kg/mol is the cryoscopic constant for water. - $m$ = molality of the solution (mol of solute per kg of solvent). **More rigorous form** (from chemical potential of water in an ideal solution): $$\Delta T_f = \frac{R \cdot T_f^2}{\Delta H_{fus} \cdot M_{solvent}} \cdot x_2$$ Where: - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant. - $T_f$ = freezing point of pure solvent (K). - $\Delta H_{fus}$ = molar enthalpy of fusion (J/mol). - $M_{solvent}$ = molar mass of solvent (kg/mol). - $x_2$ = mole fraction of solute. **Engineering significance:** 1. The lower the Tif, the **wider** the temperature range over which phase change occurs (foods freeze over a range, not at a single point). 2. **Sugar-rich foods** (jams, ice cream) have very low Tif → require lower storage temperatures. 3. **Salt-rich foods** (cured meat, brine-frozen fish) also have depressed Tif. > [!NOTE] > **Marker focus:** A complete answer must include (i) the *physical reason* (chemical potential reduction) and (ii) the *equation* with all variables defined. Missing either loses marks. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Statement of assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Food shape is an **infinite slab** of half-thickness *a*. 2. Initial temperature is uniform at $T_i$. 3. Thermophysical properties (ρ, L, k) are constant with temperature. 4. Surface heat transfer coefficient *h* is constant. 5. The freezing front is a sharp, well-defined plane. 6. All water freezes at a single temperature $T_f$ (an idealization). 7. Heat transfer is one-dimensional (perpendicular to slab faces). **Derivation (5 marks — as shown in Section 3.1):** The heat that must be removed in time *dt* is: $$\rho \cdot L \cdot A \cdot dx = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_f - T_\infty)}{x} \cdot dt$$ Separating variables and integrating from x = 0 to x = a, t = 0 to t = t: $$\rho \cdot L \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = k \cdot (T_f - T_\infty) \cdot t_{cond}$$ Solving for t (with surface resistance term added): $$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{(T_f - T_\infty)} \left[ \frac{a}{2h} + \frac{a^2}{8k} \right]$$ This is **Plank's equation for an infinite slab**, with shape factors $P = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and $R = \tfrac{1}{8}$. > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Forgetting the *surface resistance* term $\tfrac{a}{2h}$ — this gives a freezing time that is too small. Always include **both** terms in Plank's equation. Also, students often mix up the *full thickness* with the *half-thickness* *a*. KTU 2024 convention: *a* = half-thickness. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model - Part B]** **(a)** Differentiate between **sensible heat** and **latent heat** of a food system. Compute the total refrigeration load to freeze 500 kg of a meat product from 25 °C to −25 °C using the following data: water content = 0.70, initial freezing point = −2 °C, bound water = 4 % of total water, $c_{above}$ = 3.5 kJ/(kg·K), $c_{ice}$ = 2.0 kJ/(kg·K), $L$ = 333.4 kJ/kg. (7 marks) **(b)** Discuss the effect of freezing on the **rheological (texture)** and **microstructural** properties of food. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Differentiation (2 marks):** | Aspect | Sensible Heat | Latent Heat | |---|---|---| | Definition | Heat causing a *temperature change* with no phase change | Heat causing a *phase change* with no temperature change | | Detection | Measurable by thermometer | Not measurable by thermometer | | Quantity | $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q_L = m \cdot L$ | | Example in freezing | Cooling food from 25 °C to −2 °C | Water → Ice at −2 °C | **Refrigeration Load Calculation (5 marks):** Step 1: Effective latent heat: $$L_{eff} = 333.4 \cdot 0.70 \cdot (1 - 0.04) = 333.4 \cdot 0.70 \cdot 0.96 = 224.05 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 2: Sensible heat above freezing: $$Q_1 = 3.5 \cdot (25 - (-2)) = 3.5 \cdot 27 = 94.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 3: Latent heat at freezing zone: $$Q_2 = L_{eff} = 224.05 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 4: Sensible heat below freezing: $$Q_3 = 2.0 \cdot (-2 - (-25)) = 2.0 \cdot 23 = 46.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 5: Total per kg: $$Q_{total} = 94.5 + 224.05 + 46.0 = 364.55 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Step 6: For 500 kg: $$Q_{batch} = 364.55 \cdot 500 = 182{,}275 \text{ kJ} = 182.275 \text{ MJ}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step-by-step marking:** Each stage of the calculation carries 1 mark. The final batch value carries 1 mark. Don't forget to convert L to kJ/kg before arithmetic. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Effect on Texture (Rheology) (3.5 marks):** 1. **Cellular damage:** Ice crystals form first in the extracellular space (because intracellular fluid has higher solute concentration and thus a lower freezing point). The growing extracellular crystals draw water out of the cells, dehydrating them. This leads to **cell wall rupture** and loss of turgor. 2. **Toughening:** On thawing, water escapes from ruptured cells, and the product feels **soft, mushy, or weepy** (drip loss). Repeated freeze-thaw cycles exacerbate this. 3. **Hardness change:** Freezing can either **increase hardness** (in high-starch foods like potatoes) or **decrease firmness** (in fruits with high pectin). 4. **Drip loss** is the most economically important textural defect — a direct consequence of cellular structural damage. **Effect on Microstructure (3.5 marks):** 1. **Ice crystal size determines quality:** - **Slow freezing** → large extracellular ice crystals → more cell damage → poor texture. - **Fast freezing** → small intra- and extracellular crystals → minimal cell damage → better texture. 2. **Recrystallization** during storage: small ice crystals merge into larger ones (Ostwald ripening). This is accelerated by temperature fluctuations in the cold chain. 3. **Protein denaturation:** freezing can cause protein aggregation (especially in fish and meat), affecting water-holding capacity and texture. 4. **Porosity changes:** sublimation of ice during freeze-drying (or migration during frozen storage) creates a porous matrix. > [!WARNING] > **Common student pitfalls in this answer:** > 1. Writing only "freezing damages cells" — too vague. Specify *how* (extracellular ice formation, osmotic dehydration of cells). > 2. Confusing *glass transition* with *freezing point*. These are different temperatures with different physical meanings. > 3. Forgetting to discuss *recrystallization* — a frequent KTU question. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 reasons students lose marks on "Frozen Food Properties":** > 1. **Mixing up half-thickness *a* and full thickness 2a** in Plank's equation. The KTU convention uses *a* = half-thickness; do NOT double-substitute. > 2. **Omitting the surface resistance term** $\tfrac{Pa}{h}$ in Plank's equation. The full equation has *two* terms — students often write only the conduction term. > 3. **Forgetting the bound-water correction** in effective latent heat. Pure L = 333.4 kJ/kg is the upper bound; in food, the *effective* value is smaller. > 4. **Unit mismatch** — converting L from kJ/kg to J/kg only in the prefactor but forgetting the consistency check in the surface term. Always carry units throughout. > 5. **No units in the final answer** — the KTU 2024 answer script template explicitly requires units (s, min, h, kJ, kJ/kg, etc.). An answer without units is marked *incomplete*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist — Frozen Food Properties** - **Freezing point depression** in food is due to dissolved solutes; quantified by the cryoscopic equation $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$. - The **three stages** of freezing heat removal are: **sensible cooling above Tf, latent heat removal (the largest stage, ~ 60–80 % of total load), and sub-cooling below Tf**. - **Plank's equation** for freezing time: $$t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{(T_f - T_\infty)} \left[ \frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a^2}{k} \right]$$ Shape factors: slab $P = \tfrac{1}{2}, R = \tfrac{1}{8}$; cylinder $P = \tfrac{1}{4}, R = \tfrac{1}{16}$; sphere $P = \tfrac{1}{6}, R = \tfrac{1}{24}$. - **Effective latent heat** is reduced by bound/unfreezable water: $L_{eff} = L \cdot X_w \cdot (1 - X_b)$. - **Thermal conductivity** of food increases during freezing because ice is a better conductor than water (Siebel's linear mixing rule applies). - **Ice density (917 kg/m³) < water density (1000 kg/m³)**, causing ~9 % volume expansion — critical for packaging design. - **Glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the temperature below which amorphous food matrices become glassy and shelf-stable; storage above Tg leads to recrystallization, caking, and quality loss. - **Water activity (aw)** drops during freezing: ~0.82 at −18 °C, ~0.75 at −30 °C; this is the *physical* basis of microbial inhibition in frozen storage. - **Fast freezing = small ice crystals = better texture**; slow freezing = large extracellular crystals = cell damage and drip loss. - **Recrystallization** is the slow growth of large ice crystals at the expense of small ones, accelerated by temperature fluctuations. - **Plank's equation assumptions:** 1D heat transfer, constant properties, sharp freezing front, uniform initial temperature, constant h and T∞. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU tip:** When solving a numerical problem, always (a) state assumptions, (b) write the governing equation with units, (c) show substitution step-by-step, and (d) state the final answer with units. Following this template guarantees full credit under the KTU 2024 scheme valuation key.

topics.title: Freezing Time, Factors Influencing Freezing Time, Freezing/Thawing Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freezing Time, Factors Influencing Freezing Time, Freezing/Thawing Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Freezing Time — The Academic Definition

**Freezing Time** is formally defined as the total time required to reduce the temperature of a food product from its initial temperature (usually above the initial freezing point) to a final, desired storage temperature (typically around **-18 °C** to **-40 °C** at the thermal center of the product). It is fundamentally a transient, unsteady-state heat transfer problem governed by the movement of a moving solidification front (freezing front) through the food matrix.

In food process engineering, freezing time is NOT just the time to convert water into ice — it includes:
- **Pre-cooling phase** (removing sensible heat from above 0 °C)
- **Phase change phase** (latent heat removal, the longest segment)
- **Sub-cooling phase** (bringing the product below the freezing point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Freezing time is the most commonly asked numerical problem in Module 3. Plank's Equation and its modified form are **mandatory** for ESE preparation. Any question worth 7–14 marks will likely involve either deriving, listing assumptions of, or applying Plank's equation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ice Cream Tub in Your Freezer"

Imagine placing a tub of ice cream (a semi-solid, water-rich food) into a domestic freezer set at **-20 °C**. 

- The outermost layer freezes first (touches the cold air) — this is like **conductor A** in a circuit.
- Heat from the center has to travel outward through the already-frozen shell — this frozen shell acts as an **insulator** (like a woolen sweater).
- The thicker the frozen layer grows, the slower the heat can escape, because the **thermal resistance keeps increasing**.
- Eventually, the center freezes too, and the tub is fully solid.

This is the core idea of freezing: a **moving boundary problem** where the resistance to heat flow grows with time, making the process non-linear.

## 1.3 Thawing Time — Definition

**Thawing Time** is essentially the **reverse** of freezing time — the time required to raise the temperature of a frozen food product from a frozen storage temperature (e.g., **-18 °C**) back to a usable, unfrozen state (e.g., **+4 °C** to **+7 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Difference:** Thawing is generally **slower** than freezing, even under identical conditions. Why? Because the thawed outer layer is high in water (which has high thermal conductivity when liquid) BUT it traps the ice inside, and surface water accumulation can create a "wet insulating film" that resists heat transfer from warm air to the still-frozen interior. Also, ice has higher thermal conductivity than water, so removing heat (freezing) is faster than adding heat (thawing) through the same frozen layer.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (as per KTU Convention)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Food) |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Freezing Point | $T_f$ | **-1 °C to -3 °C** |
| Final Product Temperature | $T_1$ | **-18 °C to -40 °C** |
| Refrigerant/Medium Temperature | $T_a$ | **-30 °C to -40 °C** (air blast); **-40 °C** (immersion) |
| Latent Heat of Fusion | $\lambda$ | **335 kJ/kg** (pure water); **~250–280 kJ/kg** (foods) |
| Density of Food | $\rho$ | **800–1100 kg/m³** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Governing Physics — Unsteady-State Heat Transfer with Phase Change

Freezing is a **moving boundary problem** described mathematically by the **Stefan Problem** (1891). The interface between frozen and unfrozen zones moves as a function of time, governed by:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T$$

where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity. Solving this exactly is mathematically intractable for real food geometries, so **empirical and semi-empirical equations** were developed — the most important being **Plank's Equation (1913)**.

## 2.2 Plank's Equation — The Cornerstone Formula

Plank's equation is the **single most important formula** for freezing/thawing time in the KTU syllabus. It assumes:

1. Constant surface temperature (or constant heat transfer coefficient)
2. Uniform initial temperature
3. All water freezes at a single temperature (no pre-freezing zone)
4. Constant thermal conductivity in the frozen zone
5. Latent heat dominates; sensible heat is neglected
6. Negligible contact resistance

The general form of **Plank's Equation**:

$$t_f = \frac{\lambda \cdot \rho}{T_f - T_a} \cdot \frac{L^2}{k} \cdot \left[ \frac{P_s}{h \cdot L} + \frac{R_s}{2} \right]$$

Where:
- $t_f$ = Freezing time (seconds)
- $\lambda$ = Latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $\rho$ = Density of food (kg/m³)
- $T_f$ = Initial freezing point of food (°C)
- $T_a$ = Temperature of cooling medium (°C)
- $L$ = Characteristic dimension (m)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of frozen food (W/m·K)
- $h$ = Surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $P_s$ and $R_s$ = **Shape factors** (dimensionless) — depend on geometry
- $\Delta H = \lambda \cdot \rho$ = Volumetric latent heat (J/m³)

## 2.3 Shape Factors (Crucial for KTU Problems!)

| Geometry | $P_s$ | $R_s$ | Characteristic Dimension $L$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Infinite Slab** (both sides cooled) | $\frac{1}{2}$ | $\frac{1}{8}$ | Half-thickness |
| **Infinite Cylinder** | $\frac{1}{4}$ | $\frac{1}{16}$ | Radius |
| **Sphere** | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $\frac{1}{24}$ | Radius |
| **Infinite Cylinder (small)** | $\frac{1}{2}$ | $\frac{1}{8}$ | Half-thickness (approx.) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the exam-day lifeline for Module 3. Memorize it.**

| Formula | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Plank's Equation (general) | $t_f = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T_f - T_a} \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{k} \left[ \dfrac{P_s}{hL} + \dfrac{R_s}{2} \right]$ | Any geometry |
| Slab (both sides) | $t_f = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T_f - T_a} \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{k} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2hL} + \dfrac{1}{8} \right]$ | Meat slabs, fish fillets |
| Cylinder | $t_f = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T_f - T_a} \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{k} \left[ \dfrac{1}{4hL} + \dfrac{1}{16} \right]$ | Sausages, carrots |
| Sphere | $t_f = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T_f - T_a} \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{k} \left[ \dfrac{1}{6hL} + \dfrac{1}{24} \right]$ | Peas, fruits, meatballs |
| Modified Plank (Cleland & Earle) | $t_f = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta T_1} \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{k} \left[ \dfrac{P_s}{Bi} + \dfrac{R_s}{2} \right]$ where $Bi = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ | More accurate; includes $C_p$ |
| Biot Number | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot L}{k}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Thawing Time (Plank) | $t_{th} = t_f \cdot \dfrac{k_f}{k_{th}} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 + Bi_{th}}{1 + Bi_f} \right]$ | Thawing analogue |

## 2.5 Modified Plank's Equation (Cleland & Earle, 1982)

The original Plank equation neglects sensible heat. Cleland and Earle added correction factors:

$$t_f = \frac{\Delta H_1 \cdot L^2}{\Delta T_1 \cdot k_f} \left( \frac{P_s}{Bi} + \frac{R_s}{2} \right)$$

where $\Delta H_1 = \Delta H + \Delta H_s$ (effective latent heat including sensible heat pre-cooling).

The correction factor $P_s$ and $R_s$ are also redefined as functions of $Bi$, $Ste$, and $Pk$ (Stefan, Plank numbers).

## 2.6 Factors Influencing Freezing Time

The eight primary factors that influence freezing time are:

1. **Product Dimensions (Geometry & Size)** — Larger products take disproportionately longer due to the $L^2$ dependence.
2. **Initial Temperature of Product** — Higher initial temperature increases pre-cooling load.
3. **Final Temperature of Product** — Lower final temperature requires more sub-cooling.
4. **Thermal Conductivity of Frozen Food ($k_f$)** — Higher $k_f$ → faster freezing.
5. **Surface Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$)** — Higher $h$ (forced convection, immersion) → faster freezing.
6. **Latent Heat of Freezing ($\lambda$)** — Foods with higher water content have higher $\lambda$ and take longer.
7. **Initial Freezing Point ($T_f$)** — Lower $T_f$ means wider $(T_f - T_a)$ driving force → faster.
8. **Product Composition** — Fat content, salt, sugar, and water activity all shift $T_f$ and $\lambda$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Plank's equation is used in designing industrial freezers (air-blast, plate, immersion, cryogenic), calculating throughput (tons/hour), sizing compressors, and optimizing energy consumption. Reducing freezing time is critical because **faster freezing = smaller ice crystals = better texture retention**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation (Slab Geometry)
