#### **Model Solution to B-B (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
> **Glass transition temperature (Tg')** is the temperature below which the *maximally freeze-concentrated* unfrozen solute matrix of a frozen food exists in a **vitreous (glassy, amorphous, kinetically frozen) state**, characterized by extremely high viscosity ($> 10^{12}$ Pa·s) and negligible molecular mobility.

**Schematic diagram (2 marks) — describe in words since drawing text is involved:**

> A state diagram of the food with $T$ on the y-axis and solids concentration on the x-axis. Three curves: (i) freezing curve (ice line) sloping downward to the right; (ii) solubility curve (Tg' curve) extending rightward; (iii) the intersection gives $T_g'$ (typically −32 to −60 °C for foods).

**Relationship $T$ vs $T_g'$ (3 marks):**

| Condition | State | Stability |
|---|---|---|
| $T < T_g'$ | **Glassy** (amorphous solid) | Stable — molecular mobility negligible, diffusion-limited reactions halted, no recrystallization |
| $T > T_g'$ | **Rubbery** (viscoelastic) | Unstable — high molecular mobility, recrystallization accelerated, enzymatic and chemical reactions proceed |
| $T$ at $T_m$ (melting) | **Liquid** (melt) | Highly unstable |

**Key points (for full credit):**
- The closer $T_{storage}$ is to $T_g'$, the **shorter the shelf-life**.
- Storing at $T_{storage} \ll T_g'$ maximizes stability — this is the *practical guideline* for industrial deep-freezing.
- Foods with high sugar content (fruits, ice cream) have *high* $T_g'$ → require *colder* storage.
- Foods with high starch/protein content have *lower* $T_g'$ → comparatively more stable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frozen Food Storage Quality Changes**

### **A. Physical Changes**
- ☐ **Recrystallization** = growth of ice crystals (4 mechanisms: Ostwald ripening, isomass rounding, accretion, migratory).
- ☐ **Freezer burn** = surface dehydration via sublimation; prevented by impermeable packaging.
- ☐ **Drip loss** = exudate on thawing; $D = m_{\text{drip}}/m_{\text{initial}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Colour changes** = chlorophyll → pheophytin (green → brown), carotenoid fading, Maillard browning.

### **B. Chemical Changes**
- ☐ **Lipid oxidation** is the **most critical** chemical change in fatty frozen foods; measured by **PV** and **TBA**.
- ☐ Arrhenius law applies: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ with $E_a \approx 40$–$80$ kJ/mol for oxidation.
- ☐ **Protein denaturation** in gadoid fish is severe; reduced by cold-chain integrity.
- ☐ **Vitamin C** follows first-order loss; $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$.
- ☐ **Maillard browning** is $a_w$-dependent (peak near $a_w = 0.6$–$0.8$).

### **C. Biochemical Changes**
- ☐ Enzymes are **NOT completely inactivated** at −18 °C; 1–5 % activity remains.
- ☐ Critical enzymes: **lipase, LOX, PPO, catalase, pectinases, chlorophyllase**.
- ☐ **Blanching** is the *pre-treatment* to inactivate enzymes; storage only slows them.

### **D. Microbiological Changes**
- ☐ **Psychrotrophs** (*L. monocytogenes*, *Y. enterocolitica*) can grow at sub-zero temperatures in unfrozen water.
- ☐ **Spore-formers** (*Clostridium*, *Bacillus*) are unaffected by freezing.
- ☐ **Sub-lethal injury** is a major post-thaw food-safety concern.
- ☐ Freezing is **not** a sterilization process.

### **E. Glass Transition (Tg')**
- ☐ $T_g'$ is the key predictor of frozen food stability.
- ☐ $T_{storage} < T_g'$ → glassy → **stable**.
- ☐ $T_{storage} > T_g'$ → rubbery → **unstable** (recrystallization, reactions proceed).

### **F. Operational Best Practices (KTU + FSSAI aligned)**
- ☐ Store at **−18 °C or below** (FSSAI / Codex Alimentarius).
- ☐ Keep **temperature fluctuation < ±1 °C**.
- ☐ Use **moisture- and oxygen-impermeable packaging**.
- ☐ Apply **first-in, first-out (FIFO)** inventory.
- ☐ Monitor **cold-chain integrity** with data loggers.
- ☐ Conduct **Time-Temperature Tolerance (TTT)** analysis for product release decisions.

### **G. Numerical Skills to Demonstrate**
- ☐ Solve **Arrhenius two-point** problems (the most common 7-mark computation).
- ☐ Compute **first-order degradation** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ and half-life.
- ☐ Calculate **drip loss percentage**.
- ☐ Use **$Q_{10}$** to compare rates at two temperatures.
- ☐ State the **Clausius–Clapeyron** equation to explain sublimation driving force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks "Discuss quality changes during frozen storage", structure your answer as **4 sub-headings (Physical, Chemical, Biochemical, Microbiological)** with **2 examples + 1 quantitative measure** for each. This gives you a clean 14-mark response in the typical KTU ESE format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freeze Drying: Heat Mass Transfer During Freeze Drying, Equipment and Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freeze Drying: Heat Mass Transfer During Freeze Drying, Equipment and Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** is a low-temperature dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen product by **sublimation** — the direct phase transition of water from the solid state (ice) to the vapor state — under high vacuum conditions. The process is governed by simultaneous **heat transfer** to the sublimation front and **mass transfer** of water vapor through the dried porous cake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Freeze drying is classified as a *combined heat and mass transfer operation* with a *moving sublimation interface*. It is the gold-standard preservation technique for **heat-sensitive, high-value biological and food products** (vaccines, probiotics, instant coffee, herbs) because it operates at temperatures well below the freezing point and minimizes thermal degradation, oxidation, and structural collapse.

### 1.2 The Three Sequential Stages of Freeze Drying

| Stage | Name | Process | Temperature Range | Pressure |
|-------|------|---------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Pre-freezing** | Product is frozen to immobilize free water | −40 °C to −80 °C | Atmospheric |
| 2 | **Primary Drying (Sublimation)** | Ice → Vapor under vacuum | −30 °C to −10 °C | 10–100 Pa (0.1–1 mbar) |
| 3 | **Secondary Drying (Desorption)** | Bound/unfrozen water removal | 20 °C to 60 °C | 10–30 Pa |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Woolly Story":**
> Imagine a woollen sweater that got soaked in the rain. If you dry it on a heater (conventional drying), the water first heats up, then evaporates — but the sweater shrinks, felts, and loses its softness due to high-temperature damage. Now, imagine instead freezing the sweater solid at −40 °C (so all water becomes ice crystals), then placing it in a vacuum chamber. The ice will *slowly vanish into thin air*, leaving the sweater fluffy, intact, and perfectly preserved. **That is freeze drying in essence** — drying *without ever passing through the liquid phase* — preserving color, aroma, nutrients, and microstructure.

### 1.4 Why Freeze Drying is Unique

- **No liquid-vapor interface exists** → minimal surface tension damage → maintains pore structure of the dried cake.
- Operates at **sub-ambient temperatures** → ideal for thermolabile nutrients (vitamin C, probiotics, volatile aroma compounds).
- **Final moisture** can be driven down to **1–3 % (wet basis)**, much lower than air/spray drying.
- Reconstitution properties are superior due to retained porous skeleton.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:**
> **Latent heat of sublimation of ice ≈ 2838 kJ/kg** (≈ **680 cal/g**) at 0 °C. This is the energy barrier that must be supplied as heat during primary drying.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coupled Heat and Mass Transfer Problem

Freeze drying is fundamentally a **moving boundary (Stefan) problem**. A sublimation front (ice–vapor interface) recedes through the product as drying progresses. This front separates two regions:

1. **Frozen core (below)** — ice matrix at sublimation temperature $T_s$.
2. **Dried cake (above)** — porous solid through which vapor escapes.

Heat must flow **from the heated shelf → dried cake → sublimation front** while water vapor must flow **from the sublimation front → through dried cake → into the chamber**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Heat Transfer

- **Step 1:** Heat is supplied from a heated shelf (electric resistance or circulating fluid) at temperature $T_{shelf}$.
- **Step 2:** Heat flows by **conduction** through the dried layer of thickness $x(t)$.
- **Step 3:** A fraction of heat is also supplied by **radiation** from chamber walls to the product surface.
- **Step 4:** Heat reaches the receding sublimation interface and is consumed as **latent heat of sublimation**.
- **Step 5:** Sublimation temperature $T_s$ is governed by the local chamber pressure $p_c$ via the **ice vapor pressure curve** (Clausius–Clapeyron relation).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Mass Transfer

- **Step 1:** At the sublimation front, ice exerts its saturated vapor pressure $p_s(T_s)$.
- **Step 2:** Water vapor diffuses through the dry porous layer (Knudsen + molecular diffusion).
- **Step 3:** Pressure gradient drives vapor toward the chamber at lower pressure $p_c$.
- **Step 4:** Vapor condenses on a **cold condenser surface** held below −50 °C.
- **Step 5:** The bottleneck of drying is the **resistance of the dry cake** — as $x(t)$ grows, mass transfer rate falls.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{k_d \, A \, (T_{shelf} - T_s)}{x(t)}$ | Heat conduction through dried layer | W |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s$ | Energy balance at sublimation front | W |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{D_e \, A \, M_w \, (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \, x(t)}$ | Mass transfer through dried layer (kg/s) | kg/s |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{A \, (p_s - p_c)}{R_s}$ | Compact form with cake resistance $R_s$ | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{ice} \, A}$ | Receding front velocity | m/s |
| 6 | $\ln p_s = -\dfrac{\Delta H_s}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_s} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron for ice vapor pressure | Pa |
| 7 | $R_s = \dfrac{x(t)}{D_e \cdot A}$ | Resistance of dried cake to vapor flow | s/m |
| 8 | $M(t) = M_0 - \dfrac{\rho_{ice} \, A \, x(t)}{m_{dry}}$ | Instantaneous moisture content (dry basis) | kg water/kg dry |

Where:
- $k_d$ = thermal conductivity of dried cake ≈ **0.025–0.04 W/m·K**
- $D_e$ = effective diffusivity of vapor in dried cake ≈ **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ m²/s** in porous matrix (note: this is an *effective* pseudo-diffusivity, not a true molecular diffusivity)
- $\Delta H_s$ = latent heat of sublimation = **2838 kJ/kg**
- $M_w$ = molar mass of water = 0.018 kg/mol
- $R$ = universal gas constant = 8.314 J/mol·K
- $R_s$ = cake resistance parameter (KTU frequently uses this)

### 2.5 The Critical Control Variable: Collapse Temperature ($T_c$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collapse Temperature ($T_c$):** The temperature above which the amorphous matrix of the dried product loses viscosity and undergoes structural collapse during primary drying. Operating $T_s > T_c$ causes shrinkage, loss of porosity, and loss of reconstitution ability. For sucrose: $T_c \approx -32$ °C; for skim milk: $T_c \approx -26$ °C. **Always set $T_s < T_c$.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Pharmaceutical industry**: 60 % of biopharmaceuticals are freeze-dried (insulin, monoclonal antibodies, vaccines). Cost is high but quality is unmatched.
- **Space and military rations**: Freeze-dried meals are lightweight, shelf-stable for 25+ years.
- **Coffee industry**: Instant coffee retains 95 %+ of original aroma volatiles.
- **Biopreservation**: Tissue samples, microbial cultures, and blood plasma are preserved without ice-damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Coupled Heat–Mass Transfer Equation (Sublimation Flux)

### Statement
Derive the steady-state sublimation flux $\dot{m}/A$ by coupling heat transfer through the dried cake with mass transfer of water vapor through the same cake, given that the only heat sink at the sublimation front is the latent heat of sublimation.

### Given
- Dried cake thickness: $x(t)$
- Dried cake thermal conductivity: $k_d$
- Effective diffusivity of water vapor in cake: $D_e$
- Ice vapor pressure at $T_s$: $p_s(T_s)$
- Chamber (condenser) pressure: $p_c$
- Shelf temperature: $T_{shelf}$
- Sublimation temperature: $T_s$

### Step 1 — Heat Transfer Equation (Conduction through dried cake)

By Fourier's law applied across the dried cake layer:

$$
Q = \frac{k_d \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_s)}{x(t)}
$$

This is the rate (in W) at which heat reaches the sublimation front.

### Step 2 — Mass Transfer Equation (Diffusion of vapor through cake)

Using Fick's law for vapor transport driven by partial-pressure gradient through the porous dried cake (Knudsen + molecular diffusion lumped into $D_e$):

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{D_e \cdot A \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \cdot T_{avg} \cdot x(t)}
$$

where $T_{avg}$ is the mean absolute temperature of the vapor inside the cake (often approximated by $T_s$).

### Step 3 — Energy Balance at the Sublimation Front

All heat reaching the front is consumed by sublimation (steady state, negligible sensible heating of frozen core):

$$
Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

### Step 4 — Equate and Eliminate

Substituting Steps 1 and 2 into Step 3:

$$
\frac{k_d \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_s)}{x(t)} = \left[\frac{D_e \cdot A \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \cdot T_{avg} \cdot x(t)}\right] \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

Note that $x(t)$ cancels on both sides — this is the **steady-state quasi-equilibrium** characteristic of the quasi-steady sublimation model.

### Step 5 — Solve for Sublimation Flux $\dot{m}/A$

$$
\dot{m}/A = \frac{1}{\Delta H_s} \cdot \frac{k_d \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_s)}{x(t)} = \frac{D_e \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \cdot T_{avg} \cdot x(t)}
$$

### Step 6 — Closure via Clausius–Clapeyron (Vapor Pressure of Ice)

The vapor pressure of ice at sublimation temperature is given by the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{p_s}{611\ \text{Pa}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_s}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_s} - \frac{1}{273.15}\right)
$$

Since $T_s$ is set by the local heat–mass balance, the system is **self-regulating**: increasing $T_{shelf}$ raises $T_s$, which raises $p_s$, which raises $\dot{m}$, which consumes more heat — feedback loops stabilize operation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rate Equation as a Function of Cake Thickness (Falling Rate)

### Statement
Show that the drying rate declines hyperbolically with cake thickness — the classical "falling rate period" behavior unique to freeze drying.

### Step 1 — Cake Resistance Form

Re-arranging the mass transfer equation:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{A \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R_s(x)} \quad \text{where} \quad R_s(x) = \frac{x(t)}{D_e \cdot A}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute and Rearrange

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{D_e \cdot A \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \, x(t)}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate Cake Thickness

Since $x(t)$ grows as ice is removed:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{ice} \cdot A}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{D_e \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \cdot \rho_{ice} \cdot x(t)}
$$

### Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$
x(t) \cdot dx = \frac{D_e \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \cdot \rho_{ice}} \, dt
$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ to $x(t)$ and $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$
\frac{x(t)^2}{2} = \frac{D_e \cdot M_w \cdot (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \cdot \rho_{ice}} \cdot t
$$

### Step 5 — Final Form (Falling-Rate Parabola)

$$
x(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, D_e \, M_w \, (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \, \rho_{ice}}} \cdot \sqrt{t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cake thickness grows as the **square root of time**. Consequently, drying time scales as $t \propto x^2$. To halve drying time, the cake must be made **half as thick** — this is why freeze-dried products are often sliced thin (5–15 mm) or presented as small pellets/beads.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Requirement per kg of Water Removed

### Statement
Compute the theoretical minimum energy required to freeze-dry 1 kg of water from a food product initially at 25 °C.

### Step 1 — Sensible cooling from 25 °C to freezing point (0 °C)

For water: $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K.

$$
Q_1 = 4.18 \times (25 - 0) = 104.5\ \text{kJ}
$$

### Step 2 — Latent heat of freezing

$$
Q_2 = 334\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Sensible cooling of ice from 0 °C to sublimation temperature (−20 °C)

For ice: $c_p \approx 2.1$ kJ/kg·K.

$$
Q_3 = 2.1 \times (0 - (-20)) = 42\ \text{kJ}
$$

### Step 4 — Latent heat of sublimation at −20 °C

$$
Q_4 \approx 2838\ \text{kJ/kg}\ \text{(at 0 °C); at −20 °C, } \approx 2838 + 2.1 \times 20 = 2880\ \text{kJ}
$$

### Step 5 — Total Theoretical Minimum

$$
Q_{total} = 104.5 + 334 + 42 + 2880 \approx 3360\ \text{kJ/kg water}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Real-world energy consumption is **2–3× higher** (≈ 7000–10000 kJ/kg water) due to vacuum-pump inefficiencies, radiation losses, and incomplete utilization of shelf heating.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Drying Methods

| Parameter | Hot Air Drying | Spray Drying | Freeze Drying |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|---------------|
| Operating Temperature | 60–200 °C | 150–250 °C | −30 to +40 °C |
| Pressure | Atmospheric | Atmospheric | 10–100 Pa |
| Phase transition | Liquid → Vapor | Liquid → Vapor | Solid → Vapor |
| Final Moisture | 5–15 % wb | 2–5 % wb | **1–3 % wb** |
| Quality retention | Moderate | Good for powders | **Excellent** |
| Energy per kg water | 3000–5000 kJ | 4500–6000 kJ | **7000–10000 kJ** |
| Capital cost | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| Rehydration | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Typical applications | Grains, pasta | Milk, whey, coffee extract | Coffee, herbs, vaccines, probiotics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> 1. **Heat flows downward** (top-to-bottom in schematic 4.2) from the shelf, through the dried cake, to the sublimation front.
> 2. **Mass flows upward** (bottom-to-top) as vapor, counter-current to the heat, through the same dried cake.
> 3. The dried cake therefore plays a **dual role**: a thermal insulator (slowing heat delivery) AND a mass-transfer resistance (slowing vapor escape). These competing resistances govern the entire drying kinetics.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between primary drying and secondary drying stages of freeze drying.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Drying | Secondary Drying |
|--------|---------------|------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Sublimation of free ice | Desorption of bound (unfrozen) water |
| **Temperature** | −30 °C to −10 °C | 20 °C to 60 °C |
| **Pressure** | 10–100 Pa | 10–30 Pa |
| **Duration** | 60–80 % of total cycle | 20–40 % of total cycle |
| **Energy** | Governed by latent heat of sublimation | Governed by desorption enthalpy |
| **End-point** | ~10–15 % residual moisture | 1–3 % residual moisture (wb) |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is collapse temperature? Why is it critical in freeze drying?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Collapse Temperature ($T_c$):** The temperature above which the amorphous (non-crystalline) components of a freeze-dried product lose their rigid glassy structure and undergo viscous flow, causing structural collapse, shrinkage, and loss of porosity.

- Critical because operating the sublimation front above $T_c$ **destroys the pore network**, trapping residual moisture and ruining reconstitution.
- Rule: **Sublimation temperature $T_s$ must always be maintained at least 2–5 °C below $T_c$**.
- Examples: Skim milk ≈ −26 °C; sucrose ≈ −32 °C; pure water (crystalline) does not have a $T_c$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the steady-state heat and mass transfer equation for a freeze-drying process and explain why the dried cake acts as both a thermal resistance and a mass-transfer resistance. State the role of the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A freeze dryer is loaded with 200 kg of a sliced fruit product (85 % initial moisture, wet basis) at 25 °C. The product is pre-frozen to −40 °C. Sublimation occurs at $T_s = -20$ °C and chamber pressure is maintained at 30 Pa. The dried cake has thermal conductivity $k_d = 0.035$ W/m·K, effective vapor diffusivity $D_e = 1.2 \times 10^{-2}$ m²/s (effective pseudo-coefficient), shelf temperature = 80 °C, latent heat of sublimation = 2838 kJ/kg, and $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. The final moisture content desired is 3 % (wb). Calculate:
   (i) Total mass of water to be removed.
   (ii) The energy requirement for sublimation alone (neglect sensible and freezing steps).
   (iii) The theoretical minimum energy per kg of dry product. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Setup the geometry.** Heat enters from the heated shelf, conducts through a dried cake of instantaneous thickness $x(t)$, and reaches the sublimation front. Vapor leaves the front, diffuses through the same cake in the opposite direction, and exits to the chamber at lower pressure $p_c$.

**Step 2: Heat transfer (Fourier's law):**

$$
Q = \frac{k_d A (T_{shelf} - T_s)}{x(t)}
$$

**Step 3: Mass transfer (Fick's law with vapor pressure driving force):**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{D_e \, A \, M_w \, (p_s - p_c)}{R \, T \, x(t)}
$$

**Step 4: Energy balance at sublimation front:**

$$
Q = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_s
$$

**Step 5: Coupling yields sublimation flux expression** (derived in SECTION 3):

$$
\dot{m}/A = \frac{k_d(T_{shelf}-T_s)}{x(t) \Delta H_s} = \frac{D_e M_w (p_s - p_c)}{R T x(t)}
$$

**Dual-resistance explanation (3 marks):**
- The dried cake **resists heat flow** because $k_d$ is low (air-filled pores have very low conductivity, ~0.025 W/m·K). Heat transfer rate **decreases as $x(t)$ grows**.
- The dried cake **resists vapor flow** because the tortuous pore path reduces diffusivity. Mass transfer rate also **decreases as $x(t)$ grows**.
- Both effects combine: as drying proceeds, **drying rate falls monotonically** (the falling-rate period).

**Clausius–Clapeyron role (1 mark):**
$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{p_s}{611\ \text{Pa}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_s}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_s} - \frac{1}{273.15}\right)
$$
This couples sublimation temperature $T_s$ to vapor pressure $p_s$, closing the heat–mass system.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**(i) Mass of water to be removed:**

Initial moisture = 85 % wb → 1 kg product contains 0.85 kg water + 0.15 kg dry solids.

Total dry solids = $200 \times 0.15 = 30$ kg.

Initial water = $200 \times 0.85 = 170$ kg.

Final moisture = 3 % wb → Final water = $0.03/0.97 \times 30 = 0.928$ kg.

$$
\boxed{m_{water} = 170 - 0.928 = 169.07\ \text{kg water}}
$$

**(ii) Energy for sublimation alone:**

$$
Q_{sub} = 169.07 \times 2838 = 4,79,841\ \text{kJ} \approx 4.80 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{sub} \approx 4.80 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ} \approx 479.8\ \text{MJ}}
$$

**(iii) Energy per kg dry product:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{per kg\ dry} = \frac{479{,}841}{30} \approx 15{,}995\ \text{kJ/kg dry solid} \approx 16{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg dry}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat schematic the construction and working of a **batch-type industrial freeze dryer**. Label all major components and explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **secondary drying** in detail. Why is it necessary even after primary drying removes most of the water? Discuss the role of **moisture sorption isotherms** in determining the end-point of secondary drying. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Batch Freeze Dryer Construction (7 marks)**

**Major components:**

1. **Drying Chamber** — A large stainless-steel rectangular or cylindrical vessel with a transparent acrylic door for visual monitoring. Houses the shelves and product trays.

2. **Heated Shelves** — Hollow plates through which a heat-transfer fluid (silicone oil) circulates, or electric resistance heaters embedded. Provide the heat of sublimation.

3. **Vacuum System** — Comprises a **rotary vane roughing pump** (atm → 100 Pa), a **Roots blower** booster, and a **diffusion pump** for ultra-high vacuum. Maintains chamber at 10–100 Pa.

4. **Ice Condenser (Cold Trap)** — A separate chamber with refrigerated coils held at −50 to −80 °C. Captures water vapor sublimed from the product, converting it back to ice and protecting the vacuum pump.

5. **Refrigeration System** — Supplies cooling to the condenser and sometimes to pre-freezing shelves.

6. **Control System (PLC + HMI)** — Monitors shelf temperature, chamber pressure, condenser temperature, product temperature (via thermocouples), and runs the recipe (ramp/soak cycles).

7. **Loading System** — Hydraulic or manual trolley to push the loaded shelf stack into the chamber.

**Working sequence:**
1. Pre-treated product is loaded onto trays on pre-cooled shelves (−40 °C) for freezing.
2. Once frozen, chamber is evacuated to operating pressure.
3. Shelf temperature is raised; primary drying begins.
4. Vapor migrates to condenser where it freezes.
5. After primary drying, shelf temperature is further raised for secondary drying.
6. Chamber is back-filled with dry nitrogen (or air), and product is sealed in moisture-impermeable packaging.

---

**(b) Secondary Drying – Necessity and Isotherm Role (7 marks)**

**What remains after primary drying?**
- Free ice has been removed, but **bound water** (10–25 % of dry mass) remains, held by:
  - Hydrogen bonds to polar groups (–OH, –NH₂, –COOH)
  - Capillary condensation in micropores
  - Water of hydration around ionic groups

**Why secondary drying is necessary (3 marks):**
- Bound water cannot be removed by sublimation (it is not crystalline ice).
- Removing bound water requires **breaking sorption bonds**, which needs:
  - Higher temperature (20–60 °C)
  - Possibly lower pressure (10–30 Pa)
- Without secondary drying, the residual bound water would:
  - Cause **unacceptable shelf-life degradation**
  - Promote **Maillard browning** and **lipid hydrolysis**
  - Result in moisture levels (10–15 %) incompatible with long-term storage

**Moisture sorption isotherms (4 marks):**
- A sorption isotherm plots **equilibrium moisture content (EMC) vs. water activity ($a_w$)** at constant temperature.
- It shows the product is **hygroscopic**: at any given $a_w$, the product holds a certain moisture level.
- For long-term microbial and chemical stability, $a_w < 0.6$ is typically required (lower limit for mold growth is around $a_w = 0.70$; for bacteria 0.86).
- The isotherm helps determine the **target end-point moisture**: by selecting an acceptable $a_w$ (e.g., 0.2–0.3 for dry foods), the corresponding moisture content can be read off the isotherm.
- During secondary drying, moisture is monitored (e.g., by Karl Fischer titration, gravimetric loss, or NIR) until the target is reached.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing sublimation temperature with shelf temperature** — students often write $T_s$ when the question demands $T_{shelf}$. Always distinguish heat-source temperature from sublimation-interface temperature.
> 2. **Mixing up dry-basis and wet-basis moisture** — a **3 % wb** moisture equals **3.09 % db**, not "the same". Conversion: $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the drying-cake resistance term $x(t)$** — mass transfer rate is **not constant**; it falls hyperbolically. Many students treat $\dot{m}$ as constant, which is wrong.
> 4. **Ignoring collapse temperature** — if asked about operating limits, always state $T_s < T_c$.
> 5. **Treating latent heat as 334 kJ/kg** — that is the latent heat of *fusion*. For sublimation use **2838 kJ/kg** (almost 8.5× larger).
> 6. **Skipping the role of condenser temperature** — vapor must condense; otherwise vacuum pump floods. Always mention condenser held below −50 °C.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freeze Drying — Things You MUST Remember for KTU 2024**

- ✅ **Freeze drying = sublimation of ice under high vacuum**, operating typically at 10–100 Pa and −30 to −10 °C during primary drying.
- ✅ **Three stages:** Pre-freezing, Primary (sublimation), Secondary (desorption).
- ✅ **Latent heat of sublimation = 2838 kJ/kg** (not 334 kJ/kg — that is fusion).
- ✅ **Heat transfer:** $Q = \dfrac{k_d A (T_{shelf} - T_s)}{x(t)}$ — falls as cake thickens.
- ✅ **Mass transfer:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{D_e A M_w (p_s - p_c)}{R T x(t)}$ — falls as cake thickens.
- ✅ **Falling rate:** $x(t) \propto \sqrt{t}$ — cake thickness grows with square root of time.
- ✅ **Collapse temperature $T_c$:** Always operate $T_s < T_c$ to retain porous structure.
- ✅ **Clausius–Clapeyron** couples $T_s$ and $p_s$, closing the heat–mass system.
- ✅ **Sublimation front is a moving boundary** (Stefan problem).
- ✅ **Final moisture:** 1–3 % wb for stable storage; $a_w < 0.6$ preferred.
- ✅ **Equipment:** Chamber + heated shelves + vacuum pump + condenser (−50 to −80 °C) + PLC control.
- ✅ **Applications:** Instant coffee, herbs, vaccines, probiotics, blood plasma, space rations.
- ✅ **Energy cost:** ~7000–10000 kJ/kg water (2–3× theoretical minimum).
- ✅ **Sorption isotherms** determine the endpoint of secondary drying.
- ✅ **Drying time scales as $t \propto x^2$** — thin slices or beads dry much faster.
- ✅ **Counter-current flow:** Heat goes down; vapor goes up — through the same dried cake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-Liquid Extraction (Leaching): Material Balance, Equilibrium, Multistage Extraction, Stage Efficiency, Solid-Liquid Extraction Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT611 - UNIT OPERATIONS IN FOOD PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Solid-Liquid Extraction (Leaching): Material Balance, Equilibrium, Multistage Extraction, Stage Efficiency, Solid-Liquid Extraction Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Solid-Liquid Extraction**, commonly known as **Leaching**, is a mass transfer operation in which a soluble component (the **solute**) is separated from a solid matrix (the **inert solid**) by contacting the solid with a liquid solvent. The soluble constituent diffuses from the interior of the solid into the surrounding liquid phase until the concentration gradient driving force is minimized. In food processing, leaching is widely employed for: - **Sugar extraction** from sugar beet or sugar cane - **Oil extraction** from soybeans, sunflower seeds, and groundnuts - **Aqueous extraction** of instant coffee, tea solubles, and vegetable proteins - **Decaffeination** of green coffee beans using supercritical CO₂ or organic solvents - **Extraction of pectin, pigments, antioxidants** from plant materials > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *Leaching is a unit operation that involves the preferential transfer of one or more soluble constituents from a solid to a liquid solvent that is in intimate contact with the solid. The insoluble residue retains its shape and physical form throughout the operation.* ## 1.2 Nomenclature and Phase Components A leaching system consists of three identifiable components in a binary mass transfer context: | Component Type | Description | Food Example | |---|---|---| | **Solute (A)** | Soluble material to be extracted from the solid | Sucrose in sugar beet | | **Inert Solid (B)** | Insoluble solid matrix that holds the solute | Cellulose/fiber of beet | | **Solvent (C)** | Liquid medium (often water) | Water | ## 1.3 Three-Phase Output of a Leaching Process Every leaching operation produces: 1. **Extract (Solution):** Solvent + dissolved solute (overflow from solid) 2. **Underflow (Raffinate / Spent Solid):** Inert solid + retained solution (entrained liquor) 3. **Vapour (if heated):** Sometimes solvent vapours are recovered > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Tea Bag Analogy:** > Imagine dipping a tea bag (the solid matrix containing soluble tea compounds) into a cup of hot water (the solvent). The hot water gradually turns brown as tea compounds (the solute) diffuse from inside the bag into the water. The bag itself (the inert solid) remains, holding back a small amount of dark, concentrated liquid (the underflow). This is leaching in its purest, most familiar form. The longer you steep, the more solute transfers — until **equilibrium** is approached. ## 1.4 Representation Conventions for Material Balance Two standard conventions are used in KTU textbooks: - **Mass Fraction Basis:** All compositions expressed as kg solute / kg total mixture. - **Solute-Free Basis:** Quantities expressed per kg of inert solid (B), e.g., kg solution/kg insoluble solid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Convention for KTU Problems:** The **underflow** always contains a mixture of solute + solvent adhering to the inert solid. We **never assume the underflow is dry** unless the problem explicitly states it. This is the single most common error KTU students make. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Material Balance Framework The general material balance for any leaching system is governed by the **Law of Conservation of Mass**: $$\text{Mass In} = \text{Mass Out} + \text{Accumulation}$$ For steady-state operation, accumulation = 0, and the system reduces to: $$F + S = E + R$$ Where: - $F$ = Mass feed (solid + solute entering) - $S$ = Mass of fresh solvent entering - $E$ = Mass of extract (overflow) leaving - $R$ = Mass of underflow (raffinate) leaving ## 2.2 Single-Stage Leaching — Operating Line Derivation Consider a single-stage contactor where feed (F) of composition $x_F$ (mass fraction of solute) is mixed with solvent (S) of composition $y_S$ (typically 0 for fresh solvent). **Overall Mass Balance:** $$F + S = M_1 \quad \text{(Total mass in mixer)}$$ **Solute Mass Balance:** $$F x_F + S y_S = M_1 x_1$$ Where $M_1$ is the total mass of the mixture and $x_1$ is the composition of the combined mixture. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The single stage produces ONE equilibrium contact, after which the underflow and overflow are mechanically separated. The effectiveness depends entirely on the **equilibrium relationship** between the underflow and overflow compositions. ## 2.3 Equilibrium Relationship — The Underflow Composition The fundamental equilibrium relation expresses the composition of the underflow liquid in terms of the overflow (extract) composition: $$y^* = f(x) \quad \text{(Equilibrium curve)}$$ For ideal systems, equilibrium is described by a **straight line passing through the origin** (dilute solution, no solute-solvent interaction): $$y^* = m \, x$$ Where $m$ is the distribution coefficient (slope of equilibrium line). For concentrated or non-ideal systems, the equilibrium relation is **non-linear** and must be obtained experimentally. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Underflow in KTU Problems:** > Most KTU problems give a direct relationship such as: *"The underflow contains 1.5 kg solution per kg of insoluble solid"*. This is a constant underflow assumption (N), which simplifies graphical and algebraic solutions. We denote the solution retained in the underflow per kg of inert solid as $N$. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F + S = M$ | Total mass balance (mixer) | kg | | 2 | $F x_F + S y_S = M x_M$ | Solute mass balance | kg solute | | 3 | $N = \frac{\text{kg solution}}{\text{kg inert solid}}$ | Underflow constant (liquid retained) | kg/kg | | 4 | $n = \frac{\text{kg solute in underflow}}{\text{kg inert solid}}$ | Solute in underflow per kg inert | kg/kg | | 5 | $y = n/N$ | Mass fraction of solute in underflow liquid | dimensionless | | 6 | $V_n = n_A + n_C$ | Total solution in nth stage (solute + solvent) | kg/kg inert | | 7 | $L_n = V_n + B$ | Total underflow in nth stage | kg/kg inert | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{stage}} = \frac{y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{in}}}{y^* - y_{\text{in}}}$ | Murphree stage efficiency | dimensionless | | 9 | $E = \frac{\text{solute in extract}}{\text{solute in feed}} \times 100\%$ | Extraction efficiency / recovery | % | | 10 | $\frac{L_n - L_{n+1}}{V_n - V_{n+1}} = \frac{-S}{B}$ | Counter-current operating line slope | — | | 11 | $R = L_n + V_n - S$ | Recycle ratio in counter-current | kg | | 12 | $y^* = m x$ | Linear equilibrium (ideal dilute case) | — | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Leaching is industrially significant because it: - Operates at **low temperatures**, preserving heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamins, antioxidants). - Uses **food-grade solvents** (water, ethanol) — no toxic residues. - Allows **selective extraction** of target compounds with high purity. - Serves as a **pre-concentration step** before evaporation, drying, or crystallization. In KTU-endorsed food industry practice, leaching is integrated into the production of: - Refined sugar (Beet → Diffusion Battery → Lime/CO₂ Purification → Crystallization) - Edible oils (Solvent Extraction of Soybean → Miscella → Distillation → Crude Oil) - Instant coffee (Roasted beans → Extraction columns → Concentrate → Spray Drying) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Single-Stage Material Balance ### Statement of the Problem A feed $F$ kg of solid containing $x_F$ mass fraction of solute is contacted with $S$ kg of pure solvent ($y_S = 0$). The underflow retains $N$ kg of solution per kg of inert solid. The overflow leaves as extract. Derive the underflow composition. ### Step 1: Define Stream Variables Let the mass fraction of solute in the **underflow liquid** be $y$ and in the **overflow (extract)** be $x$. Both streams contact until **equilibrium** is reached. ### Step 2: Total Mass Balance (Entire System) $$F + S = E + R$$ ### Step 3: Inert Solid Balance (Insoluble solid passes through unchanged) Let $B$ be the inert solid in the feed (kg). Since solvent carries no inert solid, and extract carries no inert solid: $$B_F = B_R = B$$ ### Step 4: Underflow Composition Expression The underflow $R$ contains inert solid $B$ and solution (solvent + solute). The mass of underflow solution is $N \cdot B$. Solute in underflow = $n \cdot B$, where $n$ is kg solute per kg inert solid. Therefore: $N \cdot B = n \cdot B + (\text{solvent in underflow})$, which gives solvent per kg inert = $N - n$. The mass fraction of solute in underflow liquid: $$y = \frac{n}{N}$$ ### Step 5: Solute Mass Balance $$\text{Solute in} = \text{Solute out}$$ $$F x_F + S y_S = E x + R \cdot \frac{n}{N}$$ Since $y_S = 0$ for pure solvent: $$F x_F = E x + B \cdot n$$ ### Step 6: Final Single-Stage Equation Combining all balances yields the **single-stage leaching equation**: $$F x_F = E x + B \, n$$ Or equivalently, in terms of the operating line and equilibrium point. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Counter-Current Multistage Leaching ### Schematic of N-Stage Counter-Current Cascade $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} &\text{Feed } F \rightarrow \text{Stage 1} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Stage } N \\ &\text{Solvent } S \leftarrow \text{Overflow from } N \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow \text{Overflow from 2} \leftarrow \text{Overflow from 1} \end{aligned} } $$ ### Stage-by-Stage Mass Balance Equations **Stage 1:** Feed $F$ + Overflow from Stage 2 ($V_2$) = Underflow from Stage 1 ($L_1$) + Overflow from Stage 1 ($V_1$) $$L_1 = F + V_2 - V_1$$ **General Stage n:** $$L_n = L_{n-1} + V_{n+1} - V_n$$ **Final Stage N:** Pure solvent $S$ enters + Underflow from Stage $N-1$ ($L_{N-1}$) = Underflow from Stage N ($L_N$) + Overflow from Stage N ($V_N$) $$L_N = S + L_{N-1} - V_N$$ ### Operating Line Derivation Summing the overall mass balance across stages 1 to $n$: $$ F + V_{n+1} = L_n + V_1 $$ Since $F = L_0$ and $V_1 = V$ (final overflow/extract): $$ L_n - V_{n+1} = F - V = \text{constant} = L_0 - V_1 $$ This produces the **counter-current operating line** with slope: $$ \text{Slope} = \frac{L_n - L_{n+1}}{V_n - V_{n+1}} = \frac{-S}{B} $$ > [!NOTE] > The negative slope indicates the counter-current nature: as we move from stage to stage, $V$ increases while $L$ decreases (or vice-versa). ### Recycle Relation Rearranging: $$ L_n = V_{n+1} + (L_0 - V_1) $$ Setting $L_0 - V_1 = R$ (the recycle ratio, a constant): $$ \boxed{L_n = V_{n+1} + R} $$ This is the **fundamental counter-current leaching equation** used to construct operating lines on a Ponchon-Savarit type diagram. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Single-Stage Leaching > **Problem (KTU Model):** > 1000 kg of soybean flakes containing 20% oil (by mass) are contacted with 800 kg of fresh hexane (oil-free). The underflow retains 1.6 kg solution per kg of insoluble solid. Assume equilibrium is achieved and the overflow is pure miscella. Calculate: (a) the composition of the overflow, (b) the mass of underflow, (c) the % recovery of oil. ### Given Data - $F = 1000$ kg - $x_F = 0.20$ (mass fraction oil in feed) - $S = 800$ kg (pure hexane, $y_S = 0$) - $N = 1.6$ kg solution / kg inert solid ### Step 1: Identify Inert Solid $$B = F (1 - x_F) = 1000 \times 0.80 = 800 \text{ kg inert solid}$$ ### Step 2: Total Mass in System $$F + S = 1000 + 800 = 1800 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 3: Underflow Mass $R$ The underflow contains $B = 800$ kg inert solid plus $NB = 1.6 \times 800 = 1280$ kg of solution. $$R = B + N \cdot B = 800 + 1280 = 2080 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 4: Extract Mass $E$ By total mass balance: $$E = F + S - R = 1800 - 2080 = -280 \text{ kg}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative result is physically impossible.** This indicates that the system is **solvent-starved** — the underflow must hold 1280 kg solution, but only 800 kg of pure solvent is available, plus 200 kg of oil in the feed gives only 1000 kg of total solution. The problem as stated is inconsistent. ### Corrected Approach — Assume 1600 kg Solvent Let $S = 1600$ kg (sufficient solvent). $$R = 800 + 1.6 \times 800 = 2080 \text{ kg}$$ $$E = F + S - R = 1000 + 1600 - 2080 = 520 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 5: Solute Mass Balance $$\text{Oil in} = F \cdot x_F = 1000 \times 0.20 = 200 \text{ kg}$$ $$\text{Oil in extract} = 200 - n \cdot B = 200 - n(800)$$ For dilute/ideal equilibrium, $y^* = m x$ with $m \approx 1$ (oil distributes equally between underflow liquid and extract): $$n/N = x \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = N \cdot x = 1.6 x$$ Therefore: $$200 - 1.6x(800) = 520 x$$ $$200 - 1280 x = 520 x$$ $$200 = 1800 x$$ $$x = 0.1111$$ ### Step 6: Calculate Solute in Extract and Underflow - $n = 1.6 \times 0.1111 = 0.1778$ kg oil / kg inert - Oil in underflow = $n \cdot B = 0.1778 \times 800 = 142.2$ kg - Oil in extract = $200 - 142.2 = 57.8$ kg - Mass of extract: $E = 520$ kg → Composition = $57.8/520 = 0.1111$ ✓ ### Step 7: Recovery $$\% \text{ Recovery} = \frac{57.8}{200} \times 100\% = 28.9\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A single stage recovered only 28.9% of the oil. This demonstrates why **multistage counter-current leaching** is essential in industrial practice — it can push recovery above 95%. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection for KTU Problems | Problem Scenario | Method | Key Equation | |---|---|---| | Single stage, given $N$, find $x$ | Direct algebraic + solute balance | $F x_F = E x + n B$ | | Multistage cross-current (each stage uses fresh solvent) | Stage-by-stage algebraic | Repeat single-stage calc $N$ times | | Multistage counter-current | Operating line + graphical / algebraic stepping | $L_n = V_{n+1} + R$ | | Given stage efficiency | Apply Murphree to theoretical stages | $\eta_M = (y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{in}})/(y^* - y_{\text{in}})$ | | Find number of stages (graphical) | Ponchon-Savarit diagram | Stepping between operating line and equilibrium curve | | Find number of stages (algebraic, constant $N$) | Kremser equation | $N = \frac{\ln[(x_F - y_S/m)/(x_N - y_S/m)]}{\ln(S/(mB))} + 1$ | # 4.
